questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Stone Age, Chalcolithic Age, Iron Age
questions.questionText:
With reference to the history of India, consider the following information:
Stone Age Feature Site(s)
I. - Peleolithic
Developed
in the
Pleistocene
period or the
Ice Age
Belan
valley
II. - Mesolithic
People lived
on hunting,
fishing and
food gathering
Maski and
Chirand
III. - Neolithic
People used
tools and
implements of
polished stone
Burzahom
and
Gufkral
In how many of the above rows is the given
information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● On the basis of geological age, the type and technology of stone tools, and subsistence base, the **Indian** **Stone** **Age** **is** **classified** **primarily into three types**:
o **Palaeolithic** Age (5,00,000−10,000
BCE) o **Mesolithic** Age (10,000−6000 BCE) o **Neolithic** Age (6,000−1000 BCE)
● The **Palaeolithic Age (old stone age)** **developed in the Pleistocene period or** **the Ice Age.** It was spread in practically all parts of India except the alluvial plains of the Indus and Ganga. The Palaeolithic man of India belonged to the Negrito race and lived in caves and rock shelters. They were **food gathering** **people** who lived on hunting and gathering wild fruits and vegetables. Implements and animal remains have also been found in the **Belan valley in** **Mirzapur district** in Uttar Pradesh show that goats, sheep and cattle were domesticated around 25,000 B.C.
o **In India it is divided into three phases,** according to the nature of the stone tools used by
the people and also according to the nature of change in the climate. o The first phase is called Early or Lower Palaeolithic, the second, Middle Palaeolıthic, and the third,
Upper Palaeolithic. **So, row I is correctly matched.**
● The **Mesolithic Age represents the intermediate stage in Stone Age Culture**. The Mesolithic **man lived on hunting, fishing, food gathering, and in later stages, also domesticated** **animals.**
o The characteristic tools of this age were **microliths** (miniature stone tools usually made of
crypto-crystalline silica, chalcedony, or chert, both of geometrical and non-geometrical shapes). Microlithis were also used to make composite tools, spearheads, arrowheads, and sickles after hafting them on wooden or bone handles. o **Adamgarh, (M.P) and Bagor** provide the earliest evidence for the domestication of animals.
**Bagor, Rajasthan** on River Kothari is one of the largest and best documented Mesolithic sites in India. **So, row II is not correctly matched.**
● In northern India, the **Neolithic age** emerged around c.8000−6000 BCE. Some settlements found in south India and eastern India are as late as 1000 В.C. The people of this age **used tools and** **implements of polished stone.** They particularly used stone axes.
o In the north in the valley of Kashmir at a place called **Burzahom** at a distance of about 20 km
from Srinagar. Here unique rectangular choppers, domestic dogs buried with their masters in graves are found. o **Gufkral** in Jammu and Kashmir is famous for pit dwelling, stone tools, and graveyards located
within households. o **Other famous sites include** **Maski**, **Brahmagiri**, Piklihal (proof of cattle herding), Budihal
(community food preparation and feasting), and Tekkalakota in Karnataka. Garo Hills in Meghalaya, **Chirand** in **Bihar** (considerable use of bone implements, especially those made of antlers). **So, row III is correctly matched.** **So, in only two of the above rows is the given information correctly matched.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Nevasian Industry:**
● The Middle Palaeolithic cultural phase in India is characterised by flake-tool industries.
● In 1956, Sankalia for the first time recorded and demonstrated these flake tools occurring in association with the second aggradational deposit of the river Pravara at Nevasa (Maharastra) and then within the same context in the Godavari valley in north Karnataka.
● He called this industry Nevasian (like Mousterian, Levalloisian etc.).
**Relevance**: A 500,000-year-old tool-making workshop, potentially the oldest in North India, has been discovered in the Mangar Bani forests of Haryana.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Stone Age, Chalcolithic Age, Iron Age
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Use of copper implements
II. 	 First to use painted pottery
III. 	 Knew the art of writing
IV. 	 Lived in cities
V. 	 Used spindle whorls
How many of the above are features of Chalcolithic Cultures in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Towards the end of the neolithic period began the use of metals. The **metal to be used first was** **copper**, and several cultures were based on the use of stone and copper implements. **Such a culture** **is called chalcolithic (Stone-Copper Age)**.
o Technologically, the Chalcolithic stage largely applies to the settlements of pre-Harappans, but in
various parts of the country, it appears after the end of the bronze Harappa culture. Some Chalcolithic cultures are contemporary of Harappan culture and some of pre-Harappan cultures. The people belonging to this culture used **small tools and weapons** made of stone in which the stone blade occupied an important position. At Ahar (Rajasthan) stone axes or blades are completely absent.
● **In certain settlements copper implements are found in good numbers.** This seems to be the case with Ahar and Gilund, which lay more or less in the dry zones of the Banas river valley in Rajasthan. **So, point I is correct.**
● The **first painted pottery in India belongs to the Chalcolithic Period**, with the Ochre Coloured Pottery (OCP) culture. One of which is called **black-and-red** and has been widely prevalent. It was thrown on wheels and **occasionally painted with white linear designs**. **So, point II is correct.**
● The **people of the Stone-Copper Age (chalcolithic) did not know the art of writing**. Writing is a feature uniquely associated with the Indus Valley Civilization (Harappan Culture), which was broadly contemporary but distinct from most Chalcolithic cultures. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Chalcolithic settlements were typically rural in nature**, comprising villages or small fortified settlements. The chalcolithic people were generally not acquainted with burnt bricks, which were seldom used.
o For example, at Inamgaon, in the earlier chalcolithic phase in western Maharashtra, large mud
houses with ovens, and circular pit houses, have been discovered. **So, point IV is not correct.**
● **Chalcolithic** people knew the art of spinning and weaving because **spindle whorls have been** **discovered** in Malwa. Cotton, flax and silk threads have been found in Maharashtra.
o **Cotton was produced in the black cotton soil** of the Deccan, and ragi, bajra and several
millets were cultivated in the lower Deccan. **So, point V is correct.** **So, only three of the above** features are of Chalcolithic Cultures in India**.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Indus Valley Civilization, consider the following pairs:
Harappan Site State/Union Territory
I. - Manda Jammu and Kashmir
II. - Bhagwanpura Gujarat
III. - Daulatpur Haryana
IV. - Alamgirpur Uttar Pradesh
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Harappan civilisation was the first urban civilisation in South Asia, contemporaneous with the civilisations of Mesopotamia and Egypt. It is called Harappan civilization because this was discovered first in 1921 at the modern site of Harappa situated in the province of West Panjab in Pakistan.
● The Harappan culture covered parts of Panjab, Sindh, Baluchistan, Gujarat, Rajasthan and the fringes of western Uttaı Pradesh. It extended from Jammu in the north to the Narmada estuary in the south, and from the Makran coast of Baluchistan in the west to Meerut in the north-east. The Harappan civilisation can be classified in three phases:
o **The Early phase** (c. 3200 2600 BCE) o **Mature Harappan phase** (c. 2600−1900 BCE) o **Late Harappan phase** (c. 1900−1300 BCE)
● The area covered by the Harappan culture zone is huge, ranging between 680,000 to 800,000 sq km. Sites have been found in Afghanistan; in the Punjab, Sindh, Baluchistan, and North-West Frontier Province of Pakistan; in Jammu, Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan, Gujarat, and western Uttar Pradesh in India. **The** **northernmost site is Manda in Jammu district of Jammu and Kashmir.** **So, pair I is correctly** **matched.**
● **As many as 563 late Harappan sites have been identified in the area between the Yamuna** **and Sutlej**. Most of the settlements are small, under 5 hectares. Evidence of mud floors with post holes and hearths, mud-brick structures, storage pits, kilns, and a fire altar were found from **Sanghol in** **Ludhiana district of Punjab**.
o **Sanghol** yielded evidence of a wide range of plant remains in a late Harappan context that was
dated c. 1900–1400 BCE. The remains of hyacinth bean (sem), fruits (grape, lemon, karaunda, anwala), and opium poppy seeds were also found. o **Bhagwanpura** is located in **Haryana** (in **Kurukshetra district**) its excavation revealed an
overlap between the Late Harappan culture and the Painted Grey Ware (PGW) culture. The site was excavated by the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) in 1975, led by J.P. Joshi. **So, pair II is** **not correctly matched.**
● **Daulatpur**, located in the Kurukshetra district of **Haryana**, was excavated by Dr. U.V. Singh of Kurukshetra University starting around 1976. It is **primarily a Late Harappan site**, but its later phases show an overlap with the subsequent Painted Grey Ware (PGW) culture (associated with the Later Vedic period). **So, pair III is** **correctly matched.**
● **Alamgirpur** was partially excavated in 1958 and 1959 by Y.D. Sharma of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI). It is located in **Meerut** **district, Uttar Pradesh**, on the banks of the Hindon River (a tributary of the Yamuna). It is considered the **easternmost** **known** **outpost of the Harappan** **Civilization**. Its excavation revealed a sequence of cultural transition, with the earliest belonging to the Harappan culture. The pottery found, though distinctly Harappan (like dish-on-stand and goblets), is **generally ascribed to** **the Late Harappan Phase (c. 1900–1500 BCE).**
o **Hulas** is a late Harappan site, located in the **Saharanpur** district of **Uttar Pradesh**, was
**discovered by Shri Shankarnath of the ASI**, and was **subsequently excavated from** **1978–1983**. Excavations revealed rectangular mud structures with rammed floors and post-holes along with diverse agricultural remains, including rice (both wild and cultivated), horse gram, cowpea, and oats, offer important data on the changing subsistence patterns of the post-urban Harappan population. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, only three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs with reference to the Early Vedic society:
Term Description
I. - Vrajapati Enjoyed authority over the
pasture ground
II. - Grama Smaller tribal units
III. - Vidatha Clan-based assemblies
IV. - Bali Voluntary offerings
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Harappan culture was followed by the Vedic culture. It is called Vedic Age as its reconstruction is primarily based on using Vedic texts as sources. The Vedic corpus is generally divided into Early Vedic and Later Vedic texts.
o **Early Vedic Literature/Rig Vedic Culture** (c. 1500–1000 BCE). o **Later Vedic Literature/Later Vedic Culture** (c. 1000–500 BCE).
● Rig Vedic society was a tribal society and its people lived a seminomadic life. Aryan tribes were called Janas. The titles of the officials do not indicate their administration of territory. However, **some officers** **seem to have been attached to territories.** **The officer who enjoyed authority over the** **pasture ground is called vrajapati**.
o They led the heads of the families called kulapas, or the heads of the fighting hordes called
gramanis, to battle. **They enjoyed positions of authority in the pasture grounds and** **settled villages and not over a large territory**. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The king did not maintain any regular or standing army, but in times of war he mustered a militia whose military functions were performed by different tribal groups called **vidhatha**, **gana**, **grama**, **sardha**.
o A Grama was often a collection of families (Kulas) that formed **a mobile fighting unit or band**.
The head of this unit was the Gramani. When the gramas clashed with one another it caused **samgrama** or war. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Tribal (clan) assemblies such as the sabha, samitı, vıdatha, gana are mentioned in the Rig** **Veda.** These assemblies exercised deliberative, military and religious functions. Even women attended the sabha and vidatha in Rig Vedic times. But the two most important assemblies from the political point of view seem to have been the sabha and the samiti. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Bali** was a **voluntary offering** or tribute, usually made in kind (e.g., cattle, grain) by the people to the king (Rajan) as a mark of submission or respect. It only became a compulsory tax in the Later Vedic period. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, only three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements best describes the Upanishads?

options.a: Prose manuals containing detailed instructions for guiding priests during rituals and sacrifices
options.b: Religious scriptures containing hymns, prayers, and benedictions used for public ceremonies
options.c: Philosophical treatises that mark the final stage of Vedic thought and spiritual revelation
options.d: Appendices to the Brahmanas and are meant only for hermits dwelling in the forest

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **literal meaning of ‘Upanishad’ is ‘to sit near someone**’.  Upanishads contain philosophical speculations. Towards the end of the Vedic period (around 600 B.C.)  they were compiled largely around the Panchalas and Videha regions. These **philosophical texts criticized the rituals and laid stress** **on the value of right belief and knowledge**.
● There are 108 Upanishads, of which 13 are the most prominent. The Upanishads dwell on the Atman and ‘Brahman’, and focus a lot on the philosophy about life, universe, self, body, sacrifice, etc.
o They emphasise that the knowledge of the self or atman should be acquired and that the relation
of the atman with the Brahman should be properly understood. **So, option (c) is correct.** o **India's National Motto - Satyameva Jayate is taken from Mundaka Upanisad.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Aranyakas:**
● They are the concluding portions of the Brahmanas and deal with the mystical and philosophical aspects of sacrifice. They were written for hermits dwelling in the forest and served as a bridge between the ritual-focused Brahmanas and the philosophical Upanishads.
● They are a category of Vedic texts that form a transition between the ritual-focused Brāhmaṇas and the philosophical Upaniṣads.
● Their name is derived from the **Sanskrit word aranya, meaning "forest,**" suggesting they were compositions meant to be studied in the solitude of the forest or by recluses and ascetics.
● They are generally attached to specific Saṃhitas (Vedic collections) and their corresponding Brahmaṇas. For example:
o **Aitareya Ara****ṇ****yaka:** Attached to the Rigveda. o **Taittiriya Ara****ṇ****yaka:** Attached to the Krishna Yajurveda. o **Kau****ṣ****itaki (or Sankhayana) Ara****ṇ****yaka**: Attached to the Rigveda. **Gyan Bharatam Mission:**
● The Ministry of Culture had launched the ‘Gyan Bharatam’ mission, a landmark national initiative dedicated to preserving, digitising, and disseminating India’s manuscript heritage through a unified digital platform called the **National Digital Repository (NDR).**
**Relevance**: The first-ever ‘Gyan Bharatam’ International Conference on ‘Reclaiming India’s Knowledge Legacy through Manuscript Heritage’ from 11th–13th September 2025 at Vigyan Bhawan.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the different schools of Buddhism, consider the following statements:
I. - In Mahayana Buddhism, Bodhisattva assists all sentient beings suffering in the cycle of rebirth.
II. 	 Tipitaka is the primary scriptural source and foundational text for Theravada Buddhism.
III. 	 Vajrayana Buddhism is distinguished by its reliance on the Tantras.
IV. 	 The ‘Two Truths Doctrine’ is the central concept of the Hinayana school.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Mahayana school of Buddhism** treats Buddha as God and worships idols of Buddhas and **Bodhisattvas** embodying Buddha Nature. Mahayana believes in universal liberation from suffering for all beings (hence the ‘Great Vehicle’).
o **According** to the Mahayana Buddhism **bodhisattva** is an enlightened being who takes a vow to
remain in the cycle of samsara (rebirth) until all other sentient beings are freed from suffering. The ultimate aim of Mahayana is ‘spiritual upliftment’. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Theravada** ('School of the Elders') or **Hinayana (Literally means ‘the Lesser Path’)** is the oldest surviving school of Buddhism, and its primary and authoritative collection of scriptures is the **Pali Canon** **(Tipitaka), written in the Pali language**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Vajrayana** ( Diamond Vehicle  or the Thunderbolt Vehicle) is an esoteric extension of Buddhist thought and practice which sees itself as a quicker, more effective path to enlightenment. Its followers **believed** **that salvation can be best attained by acquiring the magical power, which they called Vajra.**
o It is characterized by its use of Tantric texts (**Tantras**) and practices that involve esoteric tools
and techniques like **Mantra** (sacred syllables), **Mandala** (sacred geometric diagrams), and **Mudra** (ritual hand gestures) for rapid realization of enlightenment. **So, statement III is** **correct.**
● The **'Two Truths Doctrine' (conventional truth / samvriti-satya and ultimate truth /** **paramartha-satya)** is a central philosophical concept in **Mahayana Buddhism**, particularly associated with the Madhyamaka school founded by **Nagarjuna**.
o It forms the basis for Mahayana's understanding of emptiness (sunyata). While Hinayana schools
do distinguish between apparent reality and ultimate reality, the formalized 'Two Truths Doctrine' as a philosophical foundation is primarily a Mahayana teaching. **So, statement IV is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Few Prominent Bodhisattvas:**
● **Avalokitesvara:** The Bodhisattva of Compassion. Avalokitesvara is perhaps the most universally revered Bodhisattva.
o It is depicted with many arms (symbolizing the ability to reach out to many) and heads
(symbolizing his all-seeing nature).
● **Manjusri**: The Bodhisattva of Transcendent Wisdom (prajna) and intellect. o It is typically shown holding a flaming sword (symbolizing the ability to cut through the
darkness of ignorance) and a scroll or book.
● **Vajrapa****ṇ****i**: The Bodhisattva representing the Buddha's power and strength. He is often seen as a protector.
o It is depicted as a powerful figure, often wrathful, holding a Vajra (thunderbolt), symbolizing
indestructible compassion and wisdom.
● **Samantabhadra**: The Bodhisattva of Practice and Meditation. He is closely associated with the fundamental practices of Buddhism, such as the ten great vows.
o It is often depicted seated on a six-tusked white elephant, symbolizing the power of
diligence and purified senses.
● **Maitreya**: The Future Buddha. He is the **only Bodhisattva recognized in both Mahayana** **and Theravada traditions**.
o It is shown seated and ready to arise, waiting in the Tuṣita heaven until the time comes to
descend to Earth and achieve full enlightenment.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the philosophical tenets of Jainism, consider the following statements:
I. - It holds that the universe has a definite beginning and a final end with respect to time, followed by a state of absolute nonexistence.
II. 	 It posits that every living being is currently in an ignorant state regarding its true, perfect nature, and this ignorance can be eliminated through self-knowledge.
III. 	 Its central tenant is Anekantavada, which emphasizes the multiplicity, relativity, and non-absolutist nature of all viewpoints and truths.
IV. 	 It emphasises that the attainment of liberation is restricted only to those who follow the ascetic path.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Vardhman Mahavira** is often credited as the founder of Jainism because he was the one who revitalized, systematized, and established the Jain community (sangha) in its present form.
● **Jainism believes** that the **universe and all its substances or entities are eternal. It has no** **beginning or end with respect to time.** The universe runs on its own accord by its own cosmic laws. All the substances change or modify their forms continuously. Nothing can be destroyed or created in the universe. There is no need for someone to create or manage the affairs of the universe. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● **Jains believe** that since the beginning of time every living being (soul) is attached with karma and also it is **in delusion (ignorant)** state of its true nature. The main **purpose of the religion is to remove** **this delusion through self-knowledge** and self-effort. This knowledge will remove karma which is associated with it from the beginning of time. When all karma gets removed, the soul becomes a liberated soul. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **core of Jaina doctrine is expressed in the principle of Anekantavada** (doctrine of manifold nature of reality), Syadavada (the theory of conditioned predication), Nayavada (theory of partial standpoints), Triratna (three gems), Pancha Mahavrata (five great vows), and Ahimsa (doctrine of nonviolence).
o Jaina **doctrine of Anekantavada is literally the doctrine of ‘non-onesidedness’ or**
**‘manifoldness’ or ‘nonabsolutism**’. It is one of the basic principles of Jainism that encourages acceptance of relativism and pluralism. According to this doctrine, **truth and reality are** **perceived differently from different points of view,** and no single point of view is the complete truth. **So, statement III is correct.**
● While the ascetic path (followed by monks and nuns) is considered the highest and most direct route to liberation, **Jain philosophy, particularly the Shwetambara tradition, believes that liberation** **may also be possible for laypersons**. Jainism believes that only through human birth can one attain liberation, but not necessarily through formal asceticism. The ultimate focus is on the complete destruction of all karma, regardless of the stage of life (lay or ascetic), though the ascetic life is the prescribed one for that goal. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Major Sects and Sub sects of Jainism**
● The Jain religion was also known as Shraman Dharma, Nirgranth Dharma, etc.
● Jain order had been divided into two major sects. o The Digambara sect o The Svetambar sect
● **The Digambara sub-sects includes:** o Bisapantha o Terapantha o Taranapantha or Samaiyapantha
o Gumanapantha o Totapantha
● **The Svetambara sub-sects includes**: o Murtipujaka, o Sthanakvasi, and o Terapanthi
**Relevance**: Vice-President C.P. Radhakrishna recently unveiled the statue of Shanti Sagar Maharaj, a monk of the Jain Digambar faith, in Shravanabelagola.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the nature of the government and
polity during the Mahajanapada period, consider
the following statements:
I. - All Mahajanapadas had a high degree of
administrative centralization under the King.
II. 	 The kingship of the state was decided through
general consent of the common people.
III.	 The division between the executive and
judicial branches was not clearly demarcated.
IV.	 The land grants were primarily given to
Brahmins with the right to collect revenue.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● According to Buddhist texts, **Anguttara Nikaya** there were 16 independent states (Mahajanapadas). During the period of Mahajanpada, the normal form of government was monarchical but they rarely presented a high degree of centralization.
o While the large monarchical Mahajanapadas (like Magadha, Kosala) were moving towards
centralization, many areas, especially the smaller territories and the **republics (Gana-Sanghas** **like Vajji and Mallas**), retained a high degree of **decentralization**. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● In **large monarchial Mahajanpads,** like Magadh, Kingship was usually **hereditary** in accordance with the rule of primogeniture. In republican states **(Gana-Sanghas like Vajjis,** **Mallas), the head (Rajan or Ganapati) was** **chosen** **by** **a** **council** **of** **senior** **members/chiefs of the ruling clans**, not through direct election by the common people or the general population. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● In Mahajanapadas, **the King** (in monarchies) or the **Assembly/Council** (in republics) **served as** **the** **supreme** **authority,** **encompassing** **executive,** **legislative,** **and** **judicial** **functions**.
o **There was no division between the**
**executive and judicial branches of** **government**. In practice, however, there were several groups of individuals whose major role was adjudication, who were assisted by a group of smaller officials. o For example, Dandadhikari during the Mauryan period was a high-ranking judicial and executive
officer, primarily responsible for the administration of justice (punishment, law, and order). **So,** **statement III is correct.**
● **The land grants** during the Mahajanpada period, was primarily given to Brahmins with the **right to** **collect revenue (taxes)**. The transfer of significant administrative, police, or judicial authority to the grantees (a hallmark of later feudalism) was not a widespread feature of the Mahajanapada. **So,** **statement IV is correct.**
o The Satavahanas started to give tax-free grants and initiated the practice of alienating royal
revenue rights and later it developed in the form of feudalism. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the polity in the Mauryan period,
consider the following:
I. - Swamin (King)
II. 	 Amatyas (Ministers)
III. 	 Rastra (Territory)
IV. 	 Durgas (Forts)
V. 	 Bala (Army)
VI. 	Kosha (Treasury)
How many of the above elements were part of the
‘Seven limbs’ of the state?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Maurya period (c. 322 BCE – 185 BCE) was marked by innovative administrative changes and an elaborate administration. In the **Arthashastra**, Kaulitya notes down the concept of **saptanga rajya** according to which, **a state comprises seven inter-related and interlocked limbs** (angas). **These** **included**:
● **Swamin (king)**: Soul of all the seven elements. According to Kautilya, the king was the **Dharmapravakta** (promulgator of social orders as he issued Rajasasana, i.e., royal rescripts and maintained ancient rules and customs).
● **Amatyas (all high officials/ministers)**: The king appointed a council of ministers to assist him in dayto-day administration.
● **Rastra (territory and population)**: Apart from Magadha with its capital at Pataliputra, the Mauryan Empire was divided into four other provinces, with capitals at Taxila (north-western India), Suvarnagiri (southern India), Tosali (eastern India), and Ujjain (western India).
● **Durga (Fort)**: The Mauryans maintained a huge army. Higher officers called **Anta-Mahamattas were** **responsible for the security of frontier forts.** Kautilya gave detailed directions for the construction of the main fort in the capital city.
● **Kosha (Treasury):** The Mauryas maintained a huge standing army and employed a large number of state officials. Since these soldiers and officials were paid in cash, the state treasury was an essential element to run the government.
● **Danda/Bala (Justice or force):** Military might require for external defense, territorial expansion, and maintaining internal law and order. The army must be well-trained, disciplined, and loyal to the King.
● **Mitra (ally)**: From the point of the **Vijigishu** (the would-be conqueror) Kautilya discusses inter-state policy. Allies provide support in times of war, help in foreign trade, and enhance the King's overall strength. **So, all of the above elements were part of the ‘Seven limbs’ of the state.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the development of coinage system in ancient India, consider the following statements:
I. - The Indo-Greek coinage often exhibited Hellenistic artistic traditions.
II. 	 The Kushana coins typically featured both the King and a deity.
III. 	 The Satavahana coins carried the motifs of nature including motifs of fauna.
IV. 	 The Western Kshatraps coins are earliest coins bearing dates given in the Saka era.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The first documented coinage in ancient India is deemed to start with 'Punch Marked' coins issued between the 7th-6th century BC and 1st century AD. These coins are called **'punch-marked' coins** because of their manufacturing technique. Mostly made of silver, these bear symbols, each of which was punched on the coin with a separate punch.
● The **Indo-Greek coinage (c. 200 BCE - 10 CE)** is famous for its **Hellenistic tradition and** craftsmanship. Their coins, particularly those of Menander and Demetrius, were **typically silver** **bilingual coins** featuring the Greek king's portrait on one side (obverse) and the image of a Greek deity, like Zeus or Apollo, on the other (reverse), along with inscriptions in Greek and Kharoshthi. This marked a significant departure from the earlier Punch-Marked Coins. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Earliest Kushan** (1st to 4th century CE) **coinage** is generally attributed to Vima Kadphises. The Kushan coins generally depicted iconographic forms drawn from Greek, Mesopotamian, Zoroastrian and Indian mythology.
o Kushana coins, especially those of Kanishka I and Huvishka, followed a consistent pattern: t**he**
**King's portrait (often shown standing or performing a sacrifice) was on the obverse,** **and a wide variety of Iranian, Greek, Hindu, or Buddhist deities (e.g., Shiva, Buddha,** **Mithra) was on the reverse**. The inscriptions were primarily in Greek script and Bactrian language. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Satavahanas (or Andhras)** were the early rulers of the region between the rivers Godavari and Krishna. Their coins were predominantly of **copper and lead**; however, silver issues are also known. **These coins carried the motifs of fauna like elephants, lions, bulls, horses, etc.** often juxtaposed against motifs from nature like hills, trees, etc. The silver coins of the Satavahanas carried portraits and bilingual legends, which were inspired by the Kshatrapa types. **So, statement III is** **correct.**
● The **term Western Kshatraps** alludes to the **set of rulers who ruled Western India between** **the 1st and 4th Century AD**. The legends on the coins were generally in Greek and Brahmi. Kharoshti too was used. The common copper coins are the 'bull and hill' and the 'elephant and hill' types.
o The Western Kshatrap coins are reckoned to be the **earliest coins bearing dates and** **given**
**in the Saka era** which commences in AD 78. The Saka era represents the official calendar of the Indian Republic. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: A special lecture series on the History of Gold Coins in India, was organised by the Department of Ancient Indian History, Culture and Archaeology, Banaras Hindu University.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Ancient Indian history, consider the following information:
Literature Author Description
I. - Mudrarakshasa Vishakhadatta
Gives details about the mechanisms of Chanakya against Chandragupta’s enemies
II. - Indica Megasthenes
Throws light on the administration, society and economy of Maurya times
III. - Saundarnanda Ashvaghosh
Provides an account of Chandragupta Maurya’s abdication and emigration to Shravanabelagola
IV. - Parishishta Parva Hemachandra
Gives detailed account of Chandragupta Maurya's life and role of Chanakya
In how many of the above rows is the information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Vishakhadatta's Mudrarakshasa** (4th or 5th century CE) was written during Gupta period and describes the political schemes and mechanisms of Chanakya against Chandragupta Maurya's enemies, securing the throne after the Nanda downfall. **So, row I is correctly matched.**
● **Megasthenes's Indica** (c. 300 BCE), written during Chandragupta's reign. Megasthenes was a Greek ambassador to Chandragupta Maurya's court, and his work, though fragmented, is a primary source for Mauryan governance, Pataliputra, and social classes.
o It provides valuable insights into Mauryan times, describing a **highly centralized**
**administration** under Chandragupta Maurya, a **society divided into seven classes**, and **an** **economy largely based on agriculture**, with trade, taxes, and a strong state presence in various sectors. **So, row II is correctly matched.**
● **Ashvaghosha** was in the court of **Emperor Kanishka (c. 1st–2nd century CE)**. The **Saundarananda** is a Sanskrit epic poem by Ashvaghosha that describes the conversion of Buddha's handsome half-brother, Nanda, to Buddhism.
o His most prominent work is the **Buddhacarita**, a magnificent Sanskrit epic poem that narrates
the complete life story of Siddhartha Gautama up to his entry into Parinirvana. **So, row III is** **not correctly matched.**  Parishishta Parva was written by Hemachandra. It gives a detailed Jain account of Chandragupta Maurya's
life, including the downfall of the Nanda dynasty, the role of Chanakya, and crucially, the tradition of Chandragupta's conversion to Jainism in his later years, his subsequent abdication, and his final fast unto death (sallekhana) at Shravanabelagola in Karnataka. So, row IV is correctly matched. **So, in only two of the above rows the information is correctly matched.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
The name of Ashoka occurs in the copies of which
one of the following types of edicts?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ashoka** was the first Indian king to speak directly to the people through his inscriptions. They were engraved on rocks, on polished stone pillars mounted by capitals and in the caves.
● **There are a total 33 inscriptions and are** **primarily classified into Major Rock** **Edicts, Minor Rock Edicts, Separate Rock** **Edicts, Major Pillar Edicts, and Minor** **Pillar Edicts**.
● The inscriptions were composed in Prakrit language, and written in the Brahmi script throughout the greater part of the empire.
o But in the north-western part they
appear in **Kharosthi script**, and in **Kandhar** in **Afghanıstan** they were written even in **Aramaic**, in **Greek** **script** and **Greek language**. o In his various inscriptions he refers to
himself by the royal title **Devanampiya** **Piyadasi**, meaning “Beloved of the Gods,” and **Dhamm****ā****soka**, meaning "Pious Ashoka. o **His personal name, Ashoka,** appears explicitly in **copies** of **the Minor Rock Edicts I**.
● **The four key sites where the name Ashoka is mentioned are**: o **Maski** (Karnataka) o **Brahmagiri** (Karnataka) o **Nittur** (Karnataka)
o **Gujarra** (Madhya Pradesh)
● **Kanaganahalli near Sannati**, a small village on the banks of the river Bhima in Karnataka, was excavated by the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) and here a sculpture-**portrait of Emperor** **Ashoka — the only available image of the Mauryan emperor** was found. **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The Ashokan inscription carved over 2,200 years ago, is now included in UNESCO’s Tentative Heritage List.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Satavahanas, consider the
following statements:
I. - Satakarni I adopted the title "Lord of
Dakshinapath".
II. 	 Gautamiputra Satakarni's military
achievements are recorded in the Nashik
inscriptions.
III. 	 Yajna Sri Satakarni issued coins with the
motif of the ship.
IV.	 Satavahana kings prominently used
matronymics as succession was matrilineal.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **founder of Satavahanas (Andhras) dynasty is known as Simuka** and he ruled from 235 BCE to 213 BCE. During the Mauryan age, Satavahanas were part of the Mauryan Empire but after the fall of the dynasty, they declared themselves independent. The **third ruler of this dynasty, Satakarni I** performed two **Ashvamedhayajna**. Satakarni I conquered western Malwa. **Naganika’s inscription** in the **Naneghat cave describes him as lord of Dakshinapatha**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Gautamiputra Satkarni (c.106–130 CE)** defeated the Shakas and also raised the power and prestige of the Satvahanas to greater heights. He also wrested Malwa and Kathiawar from the Shakas. His empire extended from Malwa in the north to Karnataka in the south, having general authority over the presentday Andhra region.
o **His achievements are described and eulogised after his death, during his son**
**Pulumayi II’s reign, in a Nashik Inscription** of his mother Gautami Balashri (written in Prakrit). In this inscription, he is described as the destroyer of the Shakas, Pahlavas, and the Yavanas, as the uprooter of the Kshaharatas and as the restorer of the glory of the Satvahanas. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Yajna Sri Satakarni (c. 165–194 CE)** was one of the last great rulers of the dynasty. His coins, often found in coastal regions, bear the distinctive motif of a twomasted ship. This design strongly suggests his patronage of maritime trade and naval capabilities. **So,** **statement III is correct.**
● **Satavahana kings** prominently used matronymics (names derived from the mother, e.g., **Gautamiputra**, **Vasisthiputra**) in their names, this indicates the prestige of the mother's lineage in the society, but it did not mean that succession was matrilineal.
o Monarchy in the state was **hereditary**, and
even though the **Satavahanas did not** **mention their fathers in their inscriptions** that primarily bore matronymics, **succession** **was always reckoned with patriarchal lineage**. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
In the context of the Satavahana administration,
the terms Kataka and Skandhavaras refer to:

options.a:
Special tax levies imposed on traders and
artisans for military maintenance
options.b:
Military camps which served as administrative
centres as long as the king was there
options.c:
Major fortified regional capitals and
permanent military garrisons
options.d:
Titles conferred upon the highest judicial
officers in the kingdom

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Kataka and Skandhavaras** were terms used in the Satavahana inscriptions as **military camps and** **settlements which served as admınıstratıve centres so long as the king was there.** These sites
served as temporary capitals or administrative headquarters for the King and his entourage (officials, ministers, army) as they traveled through the kingdom. **So, option (b) is correct.**
● At the village level the administration was placed in the hands of gaumika, who was the head of a military regiment consisting of nine chariots, nine elephants, 25 horses and 45-foot soldiers. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the history of Sangam Age,
consider the following pairs:
Term Description
I. - Arasar Ruling class
II. - Avai Royal court
III. - Manram Village council
IV. - Aimperunkulu Council to assist the king in administration
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Though the concept of varna was known, **social classes in the early Sangam period were not** **marked by acute caste distinctions**. The more relevant basis of stratification was kuti (clan-based descent groups) where there was no real restriction on inter-dining and social interaction among the kuti groups.
o **The ruling class was called arasar**, and its members had marriage relations with the **vellalas**
**(rich peasants)**, which formed the fourth caste of peasants. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The term **Avai** means **the royal court during the sangam period** where the King presided, received petitioners, held assemblies, and conducted the business of the state. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● During the sangam age the village served as the primary administrative division. The villages were run by a group of elderly people who met as a council. **Manram, Avai, Podiyil, and Ambalam were the** **village councils.** Manram appears to have occupied an important place in the politics of rural life which later developed into a system of village government in later Chola times.
o In **Pandya region, the gramam**
**served as the local administrative** **unit** and evidence suggests that it was capable of handling its own affairs efficiently and gained the confidence of the king by its rectitude conduct of affairs. **So, pair III is correctly** **matched.**
● Sangam literature like **Silappadikaram and Manimekalai**, two of the greatest Tamil epics called “**Five** **Great Assemblies” as Aimperunkulu** and “**The eight groups of Attendants” as Emperayam**.
o The Aimperunkulu (Council of Five) assisted the King in central administration. It comprised key
officials like the Chief Priest, Envoy, Commander, Spies, and the King's friends. **So, pair IV is** **correctly matched.** **So, all four of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The Tamil Nadu state archaeology department has sought permission from the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) to excavate seven new sites across the state.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Gupta Empire:
I. - It was mainly spread in the fertile land of
Madhyadesha.
II. 	 Only the ablest son ascended to the throne
with the consent of the council of ministers.
III. 	 Fa-Hien found that capital punishment was
rare in the Gupta Empire.
IV. 	 The people paid one-sixth of their cumulative
income as tax.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Gupta Empire (circa 320 CE – 550 CE) is often regarded as the “Golden Age of Ancient India” due to the remarkable achievements in art, science, polity, and literature during this period. Founded by Sri Gupta and
expanded by Chandragupta I, Samudragupta, and Chandragupta II (Vikramaditya), the Gupta period marked the unification of much of northern India under a stable and prosperous rule.
● **The heartland of the Gupta Empire was in the Madhyadesha (Middle Country),** **corresponding broadly to the Ganga–Yamuna Doab region, especially in present-day Uttar** **Pradesh and Bihar.** This region was agriculturally rich, strategically located, and culturally significant, serving as the political core of North India since the time of the Mahajanapadas. The early Gupta rulers, including Chandragupta I, established their power base in this fertile zone, which provided both revenue and manpower for expansion. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Kingship was hereditary but only the ablest son ascended to the throne with the consent of** **the council of ministers**. The princes were trained in all branches of learning and military art. Only when one of the princes proves his qualification before the king’s ministers and was popular among the people as well, he was elected as monarch. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The Chinese pilgrim Fa-Hien, who visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II (Vikramaditya), recorded that the empire was well-governed, peaceful, and prosperous. **In his travel account, he** **observed that capital punishment was rare or altogether unknown**, and that criminal cases were resolved through fines and moral persuasion rather than severe penalties. This indicates the high moral and ethical standards prevalent in Gupta society, influenced by Buddhist and Hindu principles of compassion and dharma. **So, statement III is correct.**
● Land revenue remained the main source of income for the Gupta state. Contemporary records and inscriptions, such as the Nalanda and Damodarpur copper plates, **mention that cultivators paid** **roughly one-sixth of their produce (bhaga) as land tax to the king**. This rate was consistent with the norms described in Dharmashastra texts, and it was collected both in kind and in cash. Other taxes included bhoga (tribute), kara (taxes from vassals), and vishti (forced labour). **So, statement IV is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The Gupta Empire’s administration was a centralized monarchy with decentralized execution, where local and provincial officers enjoyed autonomy in governance.
● The empire was divided into Bhuktis (provinces), Vishayas (districts), and Gramas (villages). The Gupta rulers issued gold coins (Dinars) of remarkable purity, reflecting a strong economy based on agriculture and trade.
● In literature and science, this age witnessed the works of Kalidasa, Aryabhata, and Varahamihira, marking a golden chapter of intellectual progress.
**Relevance:** Recently, the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) undertook conservation works at Gupta-era sites such as Deogarh (Uttar Pradesh) and Sarnath, which hold temples and inscriptions from this era.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
Dynasty Capital City
I. - Cholas Vanji
II. - Pandyas Madurai
III. - Cheras Uraiyur
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Sangam Age (circa 3rd century BCE to 3rd century CE) marks an important phase in early South Indian history, characterised by the rule of three powerful Tamil dynasties — the Cholas, Cheras, and Pandyas. These kingdoms formed the political core of ancient Tamilakam.
● **Uraiyur (present-day Tiruchirappalli in Tamil Nadu) was one of the earliest capitals of the** **Early Cholas**. It was known for its fertile region along the Kaveri River and its thriving textile industry, particularly in fine cotton. The ancient Tamil literature, such as Pattinappalai and Silappadikaram, refers to Uraiyur as the seat of Chola kings, including the famous ruler Karikala Chola. Later, during the Imperial Chola period, the capital shifted to Thanjavur and subsequently to Gangaikonda Cholapuram. **So, pair** **I is not correctly matched.**
● **Madurai, located on the banks of the Vaigai River, was the historical capital of the Pandya** **dynasty**. It was an important centre of Tamil learning, commerce, and religion. The Sangam assemblies (literary academies) are believed to have been held here under Pandya patronage. The Pandya rulers, such as Nedunchezhiyan and Maravarman Sundara Pandyan, made Madurai a flourishing city of trade and culture. The Meenakshi-Sundareswarar Temple stands as a testimony to its enduring legacy as a cultural capital. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **The Chera dynasty, which ruled over parts of modern-day Kerala and western Tamil Nadu,** **had its capital at Vanji (also known as Karur or Vanchi)**. Ancient Tamil texts and Roman records identify it as a major centre of the spice trade, especially pepper. The Cheras maintained active maritime links with the Roman Empire and Arabian merchants through the western coast ports such as Muziris (Muchiripattinam). The celebrated ruler Senguttuvan Chera, associated with the Pattini cult, is mentioned in the epic Silappadikaram as ruling from Vanji. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
The three Tamil kingdoms — **Cholas, Cheras, and Pandyas** — together are known as the **Muvendar** in Sangam literature.
● Their political relations were often characterised by rivalry and shifting alliances, yet they shared a common cultural identity rooted in the Tamil language and traditions.
● The Sangam literature, composed during their reigns, is one of the earliest corpuses of secular poetry in Indian history, reflecting the socio-political and economic life of the period.
● Trade with the Roman Empire, especially through ports like Arikamedu, Puhar, and Muziris, brought prosperity and cultural exchange, laying the foundation for the later rise of the Imperial Cholas in the 9th–13th centuries CE.
**Relevance:** Excavations at Keeladi (near Madurai) have revealed urban settlements linked to the Sangam Age, indicating a high level of literacy and urban planning.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Pitribhaktas, consider the following statements:
I. - They dominated the region lying between the rivers Godavari and Kaveri.
II. 	 They used the standard lunar calendar with twelve months.
III. 	 They made agraharas endowments to promote religious learning and Vedic education.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Pitribhaktas were a relatively lesser-known dynasty that emerged in the eastern Deccan region after the decline of the Ikshvakus and before the rise of the Vishnukundins, around the 4th to 5th century CE. Their rule is known primarily through epigraphic sources found in Odisha and Andhra Pradesh.
● **The Pitribhaktas established their authority over the region between the Mahanadi and the** **Krishna rivers**, covering parts of modern-day Odisha and northern Andhra Pradesh. This area served as a transitional zone between northern and southern India, witnessing cultural and administrative exchange. Their inscriptions, found in Jaugada, Ganjam, and Srikakulam districts, attest to their political control and influence in this belt. They succeeded the Matharas and were contemporaries of the early Vishnukundins. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The Pitribhakta period marks a stage when lunar calendrical systems gained formalised use in regional administration and record-keeping. **Inscriptions from this era begin to mention months and** **tithis based on the lunar cycle, reflecting the growing standardisation of twelve lunar** **months as the basis of the year**. This development was part of a broader trend in early Indian astronomy and time-reckoning, aligning religious and agricultural activities with celestial observations. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **The Pitribhaktas followed the well-established tradition of agrahara grants**, wherein villages or their revenues were donated to Brahmanas. These grants were often made tax-free and recorded in copper-plate inscriptions. The purpose was to promote religious learning, temple maintenance, and Vedic education. Such agraharas later became centres of cultural and intellectual activity, influencing the development of temple-based learning systems seen in the later Chola and Vijayanagara periods. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
The **agrahara system** played a vital role in linking land administration with religious and educational institutions in early India.
● These grants often became the nuclei of Brahmana settlements and later evolved into temple towns (agrahara nagaras).
● The Pitribhaktas’ inscriptions are among the earliest to mention such endowments in the eastern Deccan, showing how regional dynasties adapted pan-Indian religious and administrative practices.
● Moreover, their inscriptions also reflect the transition from early historic to early medieval polity, marked by localised rule, agrarian expansion, and growing Sanskritization of society.
**Relevance:** The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) and the State Archaeology Departments have been documenting inscriptions related to early medieval dynasties, including the Pitribhaktas, Matharas, and Vishnukundins.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText: In ancient Bengal region, Dandabhukti was known as a:

options.b: Type of land grant to Brahmanas
options.d: Fortress town on the western frontier

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● During early medieval India, the subcontinent witnessed the rise of regional administrative divisions known as bhuktis, vishayas, and mandalas. These administrative units were essential for maintaining political control, revenue collection, and law enforcement. In ancient Bengal, under dynasties such as the Guptas, Palas, and Senas, several bhuktis served as major administrative regions.
● **Among them, Dandabhukti held strategic and economic importance as a prominent** **territorial division that linked the regions of Bengal and Odisha**. Dandabhukti was one of the important territorial subdivisions (bhuktis) of ancient Bengal, mentioned in several inscriptions and copper-plate grants of the Gupta, Pala, and Sena periods.
● Geographically, it is believed to have comprised parts of southern West Bengal, particularly in the modern districts of Midnapore (Paschim Medinipur) and adjoining areas near the Subarnarekha River.
● The term bhukti in early medieval Indian administration denoted a large province governed by a Uparika or governor appointed by the king.
● Dandabhukti’s location gave it both strategic value—as a frontier between Bengal and Kalinga—and economic significance due to its fertile lands and trade routes connecting the eastern coast. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The Dandabhukti region also finds mention in the Bengal copper-plate inscriptions, including those of Devapala and Mahipala, indicating its inclusion in the Pala Empire.
● The name “Dandabhukti” is derived from danda (meaning boundary or frontier) and bhukti (province), symbolising its position as a frontier administrative region between Bengal and Kalinga
**Relevance:** Excavations and epigraphical studies in the Dantan and Tamluk regions have yielded artefacts and inscriptions that are believed to be associated with the Dandabhukti area.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the ancient kingdom of Kamarupa:
I. - It finds mention in the Allahabad Pillar Inscription of Samudragupta.
II. 	 The kings adopted the title Varman.
III. 	 It was visited by Hsuan Tsang during the reign of Bhaskaravarman.
How many of the statements given above are not correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The ancient kingdom of Kamarupa represents the earliest known political entity in the region of present-day Assam and parts of North Bengal. Flourishing roughly between the 4th and 12th centuries CE, Kamarupa played a key role in integrating the eastern frontier of the Indian subcontinent into the mainstream of Indian civilisation.
● The Allahabad Pillar Inscription, composed by Harishena, the court poet of Samudragupta, provides a list of frontier kingdoms and tribal states that acknowledged Samudragupta’s supremacy. **Among these** **frontier states, Kamarupa is explicitly mentioned as one of the “Pratyanta Rajyas” (border** **kingdoms)** that offered homage and presents to the Gupta emperor. This reference confirms the political existence of Kamarupa by the 4th century CE and indicates its recognition by the imperial Guptas as an important eastern frontier region. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The earliest historical dynasty of Kamarupa was the Varman dynasty, founded by Pushyavarman in the 4th century CE. **The rulers of this line bore the suffix “Varman”, following the example of** **contemporary dynasties influenced by Gupta political traditions.** Successors such as Bhaskaravarman and Mahabhutivarman continued this title, emphasising royal lineage and Kshatriya identity. The Varman title remained an enduring feature of Kamarupa kingship even in later dynasties like the Mlechchha and Pala rulers who claimed descent or legitimacy from the Varmans. **So, statement II** **is correct.**
● **The famous Chinese Buddhist monk Hsuan Tsang (Xuanzang) visited Kamarupa in the 7th** **century CE during the reign of Bhaskaravarman** (c. 600–650 CE). In his travel records, he described the region as a prosperous kingdom with numerous temples, learned Brahmanas, and followers
of both Hinduism and Buddhism. Hsuan Tsang s account also highlighted Bhaskaravarman s cordial relations with Harshavardhana of Kanauj, indicating the political prominence of Kamarupa in contemporary north Indian politics. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Kamarupa’s** political history is divided into three main dynasties — the Varmans, the Mlechchhas, and the Palas of Kamarupa.
● Its capital shifted over time from Pragjyotishpura (modern Guwahati) to Haruppeswara (near Tezpur) and later to Durjaya.
● The region played an important intermediary role between northern India and Southeast Asia, facilitating trade and cultural exchange through the Brahmaputra valley.
**Relevance:** The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) has been working at sites such as Tezpur, Guwahati (Pragjyotishpura), and Nagaon, which are believed to have been ancient Kamarupa capitals.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Emperor Harshavardhana and his reign:
I. - Ujjain transformed from a military camp to the imperial capital of Harsha.
II. 	 Banabhatta, the author of Harshacharita, was his court poet.
III. 	 He convened assemblies at Kanauj and Prayag to propagate Mahayana Buddhism.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The reign of Harshavardhana (c. 606–647 CE) marks the last major phase of political unity in northern India before the onset of regional fragmentation in the early medieval period. Harsha, belonging to the Pushyabhuti dynasty, succeeded his brother Rajyavardhana and ruled over a vast empire extending from Punjab to Bengal. His rule was notable for political consolidation, religious tolerance, and cultural efflorescence.
● Following the decline of Pataliputra—the imperial capital of the Mauryas and Guptas—the centre of political authority in northern India shifted westward to Kanauj (modern Kannauj in Uttar Pradesh). **Harsha made Kanauj his imperial capital, turning it into one of the foremost political,** **cultural, and religious centres of early medieval India**. The decline of older urban centres led to the rise of military camps or **skandhavaras**, which evolved into new political hubs; Kanauj emerged as the most prominent among them. Later dynasties such as the Gurjara-Pratiharas and Palas also vied for control over Kanauj, recognising its strategic and symbolic importance. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● **Banabhatta, one of the greatest prose writers in Sanskrit, served as the court poet and** **biographer of Emperor Harsha.** His celebrated work, the Harshacharita, is an invaluable literary and historical source that details Harsha’s ancestry, early life, and achievements in a semi-biographical style. The text combines historical fact with ornate Sanskrit prose, illustrating the cultural refinement of Harsha’s court. Banabhatta also authored the romantic prose work Kadambari, further enhancing his reputation as a master of Sanskrit literature. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Harsha was known for his eclectic religious outlook—though a Shaiva by birth, he became a patron of Mahayana Buddhism later in life. To propagate Buddhist ideals and foster inter-religious dialogue, **he** **convened a grand religious assembly at Kanauj, where Buddhist, Jain, and Brahmanical** **scholars debated doctrinal matters**. The Chinese traveller Hsuan Tsang attended this assembly and recorded its proceedings in detail. Following this, Harsha held a quinquennial religious assembly at Prayag (modern Allahabad), where he performed charitable distributions (Dana Mahotsava) to support religious institutions and the poor. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The rise of Kanauj under Harsha marked the beginning of what historians later termed the “Age of Kanauj”, when successive dynasties such as the Gurjara-Pratiharas, Palas, and Rashtrakutas contested for its control during the Tripartite Struggle (8th–9th centuries CE).
● Harsha’s reign also saw advances in administration, literature, and diplomacy.
● His empire was organised into bhuktis (provinces) and vishayas (districts), with efficient governance supported by officers like mahamatras and kumaramatyas.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Second Historical Phase (circa AD 300–750) in the regions south of the Vindhyas, consider the following statements:
I. - It was marked by the beginning of the construction of stone temples in the peninsula.
II. 	 Sanskrit was discontinued and regional languages came to be used as the official and administrative language.
III. 	 It was characterised by the proliferation of land grants to temples and monasteries.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The period between circa AD 300–750, often termed as the **Second Historical Phase** in Peninsular India, marked a significant transformation in the socio-political and cultural life of the southern regions. This era witnessed the emergence of regional kingdoms, consolidation of Brahmanical influence, rise of temple architecture, and the institutionalization of Sanskrit as the elite and administrative language.
● **During this phase, the practice of building stone temples became a defining feature of South** **Indian polity and culture**. The early dynasties like the Pallavas, Chalukyas, and Pandyas began the construction of structural temples using stone instead of perishable materials. Notable examples include the **rock-cut temples of Mahendravarman I at Mandagapattu and the shore temples of** **Mahabalipuram built by the Pallavas**. This development signified the transition from simple rock shelters to sophisticated architectural forms devoted to Hindu deities, especially Vishnu and Shiva. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **The period witnessed the dominance of Sanskrit as the language of administration, religion,** **and high culture in the peninsular region**. Royal inscriptions, land grant charters, and religious texts were composed primarily in Sanskrit, although local languages like Tamil, Kannada, and Telugu began to evolve in literary forms later. This reflected the cultural integration between the north and the south. The Pallava and Chalukya rulers used Sanskrit widely in their royal records. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● **A distinct feature of this phase was the extensive grant of lands and villages (agraharas) to** **Brahmanas, temples, and monasteries.** These grants were usually tax-free and aimed at promoting religious learning and agricultural expansion. Copper plate inscriptions from this era mention such grants in detail, showing the beginning of feudal relationships and the decentralization of authority. This practice also contributed to the spread of agriculture and Sanskrit learning in remote areas. **So, statement III** **is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, several ancient temple complexes and inscriptions from the Pallava and Chalukya periods (e.g., at Mamallapuram and Badami) have been included in the list of UNESCO World Heritage tentative sites. The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) has also undertaken conservation and digital documentation of early stone temples and Sanskrit copper plate charters, shedding new light on the sociopolitical structures of early medieval South India.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Which of the following provisions were provided for in the Indian Independence Act, 1947:
I. - Replaced Viceroy with President
II. 	 Dominion status for India and Pakistan
III. 	 Transfer of full legislative sovereignty to Constituent Assemblies
IV. 	 Provision for a joint military command for both Dominions
V. 	 End of British parliamentary oversight over Indian affairs
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Indian Independence Act of 1947 was the final legislative instrument enacted by the British Parliament to give effect to the partition of India and the creation of two independent Dominions—India and Pakistan. Coming into force on 15 August 1947, the Act marked the legal end of British rule in India and transferred full sovereignty to the respective Constituent Assemblies.
● **The Indian Independence Act did not abolish the office of the Viceroy immediately.** Rather, the Viceroy was redesignated as the Governor-General for each Dominion, to be appointed (initially) by the British Crown but functioning on the advice of the respective Constituent Assembly. Thus, the office continued, though with a modified constitutional role. **So, point I is not correct.**
● A central provision of the Act was the establishment of **India and Pakistan as two separate** **Dominions**, each with autonomy comparable to that of other self-governing dominions of the British Commonwealth at the time. Both Dominions were free to withdraw from the Commonwealth in the future. **So, point II is correct.**
● The Act explicitly provided that the **Constituent Assemblies of India and Pakistan would** **exercise full sovereign authority**, empowered to make laws—including laws that could modify or repeal provisions of the Act itself. This marked the complete end of British legislative supremacy over Indian affairs. **So, point III is correct.**
● **The Act did not contain any provision mandating a joint military command.** While the British initially oversaw the division of armed forces, and some coordination existed temporarily during the transition, this was an administrative arrangement and **not a clause in the Indian Independence** **Act**. Each Dominion was constitutionally free to structure its own defence forces. **So, point IV is not** **correct.**
● One of the most important consequences of the Act was that **the British Parliament could no** **longer legislate for India or Pakistan**. All treaties, laws, and administrative control from Britain ceased unless adopted voluntarily by the Dominion governments. **So, point V is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following temples of early medieval
South India:
I. - The Seven Ratha Temples
II. 	 The Shore Temple
III. 	 The Kailashanath Temple
IV. 	 The Papanatha Temple
V. 	 The Virupaksha Temple
How many of the above are structural temples
constructed using masonry techniques?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The evolution of temple architecture in South India marks one of the most significant developments in early medieval Indian art and culture. Initially, temples were rock-cut, carved directly out of solid rock faces, such as the caves and monolithic rathas of the Pallava period. Gradually, this tradition transitioned into the construction of **structural temples**, which were **built using stone blocks arranged systematically**.
● **The Seven Rathas,** also known as the Pancha Rathas of Mahabalipuram (though there are seven), were built during the reign of the Pallava king Narasimhavarman I (7th century AD). **These temples were** **monolithic rock-cut structures**, carved from single granite boulders rather than assembled from separate stone blocks. Each ratha is named after a Pandava hero, representing different architectural prototypes. Although they are stylistically significant precursors to later Dravidian temples, they remain rock-cut and not structural. **So, point I is not correct.**
● **The Shore Temple at Mahabalipuram, built by Narasimhavarman II Rajasimha in the early** **8th century AD, marks the transition from rock-cut to structural architecture**. It is one of the earliest examples of a structural temple, constructed using cut stones arranged and cemented rather
than carved from a single rock. The temple complex consists of shrines dedicated to Shiva and Vishnu and is notable for its coastal location overlooking the Bay of Bengal. It also symbolises the culmination of Pallava architectural innovation. **So, point II is correct.**
● **The Kailashanath Temple at Kanchipuram, also built by Narasimhavarman II Rajasimha, is** **a structural temple** constructed using sandstone. It represents the finest example of early Dravidian style under the Pallavas, featuring a pyramidal tower (vimana) and elaborate carvings of Shaivite themes. The temple’s architectural precision, intricate sculptures, and use of structural stonework make it one of the earliest fully developed examples of South Indian temple architecture. **So, point III is correct.**
● **The Papanatha Temple at Pattadakal, built by the Early Chalukyas in the 7th century AD,** **is primarily a structural temple** but exhibits transitional features between the Dravidian and Nagara styles. It is made of stone masonry and includes a sanctum, vestibule, and pillared hall with ornate carvings. Though it combines stylistic influences, it firmly belongs to the structural phase of temple building in Deccan India. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **The Virupaksha Temple at Pattadakal**, commissioned by Queen Lokamahadevi to commemorate her husband Vikramaditya II’s victory over the Pallavas in the 8th century AD, **is also a structural** **temple**. It was modelled after the Kailasanatha Temple of Kanchipuram and represents the peak of Chalukyan craftsmanship. The temple’s well-laid plan, use of carved stone blocks, and detailed sculptures of deities illustrate mature structural architecture in stone. **So, point V is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
1. The transition from rock-cut to structural temples marks a defining point in Indian architecture
— showcasing the evolution of artistic and engineering capabilities.
2. The Pallavas pioneered this transformation, which was later perfected by the Chalukyas,
Rashtrakutas, and Cholas.
3. Rock-cut temples are carved out of existing rock (e.g., Ellora, Ajanta, and Mahabalipuram Rathas),
while structural temples are built by assembling cut stones.
4. The Dravidian architectural style, characterised by the vimana and mandapa structure, evolved
fully during this period and influenced later South Indian temple complexes such as the Brihadisvara Temple at Thanjavur.
5. These developments marked the integration of religion, art, and polity,
**Relevance:** Recently, the Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram, including the Shore Temple and the Rathas, have been under restoration and digital conservation by the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI).

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
The concepts of Prakriti and Purusha are central
to which one of the following schools of Indian
philosophy?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Samkhya system of philosophy is one of the six orthodox (āstika) schools of Indian philosophy that trace their origin to the Vedas. Traditionally attributed to Sage Kapila, **Samkhya is a dualistic** **philosophy that explains the universe and human existence through two fundamental,** **eternal principles — Purusha (consciousness or spirit) and Prakriti (matter or primordial** **nature).**
● According to this school, the universe originates from the interaction of these two eternal realities. Prakriti represents the unconscious, active principle composed of three gunas — sattva, rajas, and tamas — while Purusha is the passive, conscious principle or the observer. The evolution of the cosmos and
all forms of life arises when Prakriti comes into contact with Purusha, leading to manifestation through successive stages — intellect (mahat), ego (ahamkara), mind (manas), and the senses. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Samkhya is closely linked with Yoga philosophy, as both accept the dualism of Purusha and Prakriti; however, Yoga (as codified by Patanjali) adds the concept of Ishvara (God) and prescribes a practical discipline for attaining liberation.
● The Samkhya Karika by Ishvarakrishna (4th century CE) is the principal text of this system.
● The 25 tattvas (principles) of Samkhya represent stages of cosmic evolution, beginning with Prakriti and culminating in Purusha.
**Relevance:** The Samkhya philosophy and its concepts of Prakriti and Purusha have gained renewed attention in India through the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, which emphasizes integrating Indian Knowledge Systems (IKS) into higher education curricula.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Work/ Commentary Philosopher
I. - Brahmasutra Ramanujacharya
II. - Sri Bhashya Badrayana
III. - Prasthanatraya
Bhashyam Adi Shankaracharya
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The philosophical foundations of Hindu thought are deeply embedded in the Darshana systems (schools of philosophy), each interpreting the Vedas and Upanishads through different metaphysical and logical frameworks.
● **The Brahmasutra, also known as Vedanta Sutra, was composed by Maharshi Badrayana** (Vyasa) around the early centuries CE. It systematically organizes and interprets the philosophical ideas of the Upanishads in a concise aphoristic style (sutras). **The text serves as the foundational treatise** **of Vedanta philosophy**, establishing metaphysical discussions on Brahman (the ultimate reality), Atman (the self), and the relationship between the two. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **The Sri Bhashya is the celebrated commentary written by Ramanujacharya** (11th–12th century CE) on the Brahmasutra of Badrayana. **Through this work, Ramanujacharya expounded** **his doctrine of Vishishtadvaita Vedanta** (Qualified Non-dualism), which reconciles the unity of Brahman with the real existence of the individual souls and the material world. In this view, the universe and souls are real but are attributes of Brahman. This commentary played a central role in defining the Sri Vaishnava philosophical tradition. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **Adi Shankaracharya (8th century CE)** wrote authoritative commentaries known as **Bhashyas on** **the Prasthanatraya—**that is, the Upanishads, the Bhagavad Gita, and the Brahmasutra. Collectively known as **The Prasthanatraya Bhashyam,** these works form **the doctrinal basis of Advaita** **Vedanta** (Non-dualism), which holds that Brahman alone is real, and the world of multiplicity is an illusion (Maya). His interpretation unified Vedic philosophy with monistic metaphysics and remains one of the most influential schools of Indian philosophy. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The Vedanta tradition has three major sub-schools — Advaita (non-dualism) by
Shankaracharya, Vishishtadvaita (qualified non-dualism) by Ramanujacharya, and Dvaita (dualism) by Madhvacharya.
● While all three accept the authority of the Vedas and the Brahmasutra, they differ in interpreting the relationship between Brahman, Jiva (individual soul), and Jagat (world).
**Relevance:** Events such as Shankaracharya Jayanti and seminars on Vedanta philosophy organized by institutions like Indian Council of Philosophical Research (ICPR) have brought these classical thinkers into academic and cultural discussion.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the ancient centers of learning in South and Southeast Asia, consider the following statements:
I. - Champa in Vietnam was a great centre of education in the Vedas and Dharmashastras.
II. 	 Borobudur in Indonesia has the largest Buddhist temple in the world.
III. 	 Kamboja in the northwestern frontier of the Indian subcontinent developed as a centre of Sanskrit learning.
How many of the above statements are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Ancient India and its neighbouring regions were renowned for their vibrant intellectual and cultural exchanges, with centers of learning flourishing not only within the subcontinent but also in Southeast Asia. The spread of Indian religion, language, and educational traditions — particularly through maritime trade and cultural contacts — led to the establishment of institutions that promoted Vedic, Buddhist, and Sanskrit studies.
● The ancient kingdom of **Champa**, located in present-day **Central and Southern Vietnam**, had close cultural and religious ties with India. Historical evidence suggests that the region was deeply influenced by **Hinduism**, particularly the **Shaiva** and **Vaishnava** traditions. Champa was known for its study of **Vedas, Dharmashastras, and Sanskrit literature**, indicating that Indian religious and educational systems had a deep-rooted presence there. Inscriptions and temple records reveal that learned Brahmanas from India settled in Champa and contributed to its intellectual growth. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● The **Borobudur temple**, located in **Central Java, Indonesia**, is indeed the largest and most magnificent **Buddhist temple** in the world. Constructed during the **Sailendra dynasty** (8th–9th century CE), it represents the zenith of Mahayana Buddhist art and architecture. It was designated a **UNESCO World Heritage Site** in 1991 following a massive restoration project in the 1970s and 80s. Borobudur’s construction demonstrates the strong **cultural and religious influence of India** in Southeast Asia during the early medieval period. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The ancient region of **Kamboja**, located near the northwestern frontier of the Indian subcontinent (modern Afghanistan–Punjab region), is mentioned in both **Vedic** and **epic literature**.
