● **Malik Muhammad Jaisi** is famously the author of the **poem Padmavat**, written in the **Awadhi** dialect of **Hindi**. **Padmavat is an allegorical epic poem detailing the historical siege of Chittor** **by Alauddin Khalji**, intertwined with the romantic story of Ratan Sen and Padmini. He also authored the Akhrawat (about the creation of the world), a philosophical and theoretical text. **So, pair II is** **correctly matched.**
● **Ibrahim Adil Shah II (r. 1580–1627 CE) of Bijapur authored the Kitab-i-Nauras and is known** **as "Jagadguru"** (World Teacher) for his universalist outlook. Kitab-i-Nauras was a compilation of poems and songs, composed in Dakhini, primarily written to introduce the theory of Indian Ragas and Raginis to Dakhani culture, dedicated to Hindu deities and Sufi saints. **So, pair III is correctly** **matched.**
● **Tabaqat-i-Nasiri was authored by Minhaj-i-Siraj (c. 1193 – 1266 CE)**, a historian who served under Sultan **Nasiruddin Mahmud (reigned: 1246–1266 CE)** of Mamluk dynasty. **The Tabaqat-i-** **Nasiri** is regarded as the most reliable and fundamental primary source for the history of the establishment and early decades of the Delhi Sultanate (1206–1260 CE).
o **Ziauddin Barani** (c. 1285 – c. 1357 CE) is credited with **writing Tarikh-i-Firoz Shahi**
**(History of Firoz Shah)** is a historical chronicle that covers the period of the Delhi Sultanate from the reign of Sultan Balban (accession 1266 CE) up to the sixth regnal year of Sultan Firoz Shah Tughlaq (c. 1357 CE). **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **So, only three of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Performing Arts (Dance, Drama, Music, Martial Arts)
questions.questionText: The musical instruments such as the rabab and sarangi were introduced into India by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The introduction of new musical instruments and styles into India has historically been linked to the cultural exchanges that occurred during various empires. The medieval period, particularly after the establishment of the Delhi Sultanate, marked a significant phase in the synthesis of Persian, Central Asian, and Indian musical traditions.
● **The** **rabab** **and** **sarangi** are stringed musical instruments that became integral to North Indian classical music.
● The rabab, a short-necked lute, was originally a Central Asian instrument that reached India with the **Turkish**, particularly during the early Delhi Sultanate period (13th century).
● **The sarangi**, though often linked to indigenous development, is believed to have evolved **under the** **influence of the rabab and similar Persian instruments brought by the Turks.** It gained prominence as an accompanying instrument for vocal music in the Mughal period, but its origin lies in the earlier cultural transmissions initiated by the Turks.
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The rabab was known in Central Asia and Afghanistan long before its arrival in India. It became a favoured instrument in Sufi and courtly music traditions.
● The sarangi, derived from the term meaning “a hundred colours,” symbolises the range of tones it can produce. It became central to Hindustani classical vocal accompaniment.
● Notable medieval musicians such as Amir Khusrau are credited with popularising instruments and musical forms that merged Turkic and Indian elements.
**Relevance:** Recently, traditional Indian musical instruments have gained renewed attention through Sangeet Natak Akademi’s efforts to preserve classical instruments, including the sarangi.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements about Sher Shah Suri is not correct?

options.a: He restored the Grand Trunk Road from the river Indus in the West to Sonargaon in Bengal.
options.b: He built sarais for the convenience of travellers.
options.c: He introduced the silver Rupiya and the copper Dam.
options.d: He abolished the system of local officers known as Amins to centralise revenue collection.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Sher Shah Suri,** the founder of the **Suri dynasty,** ruled North India from 1540 to 1545 after displacing the Mughal Emperor Humayun. Though his reign was brief, his administrative and economic reforms left a lasting legacy, laying the foundation for a more efficient governance model that was later adopted by the Mughals.
● Sher Shah Suri is credited with restoring and expanding the ancient trade route known as the **Uttarapath**, which connected the northwestern regions of India with Bengal. He **renovated and** **extended this road from the Indus River in the west (modern-day Pakistan) to Sonargaon** **near Dhaka in Bengal**, facilitating trade, communication, and military movement across the empire.
● Along the Grand Trunk Road, **Sher Shah established numerous sarais (rest houses) at regular** **intervals** to provide shelter, food, and safety for travellers, merchants, and officials. Each sarai also served as a communication and administrative centre, housing postal runners and security guards.
● Sher Shah Suri standardised the Indian currency system by **introducing the silver coin called Rupiya,** **weighing 178 grains (approximately 11.53 grams), along with the copper Dam and the gold** **Mohur**. His well-regulated tri-metallic coinage system formed the basis of the Mughal and later British Indian monetary system.
● **Sher Shah did not abolish the Amins; rather, he made use of local officers and village** **records to assess and collect revenue effectively**. His land revenue system was based on careful measurement of land, classification of soil, and fixation of revenue rates. Local officials such as Amins, Kanungos, and Patwaris were integral to his system for maintaining accuracy and preventing corruption. **Therefore, option (d) is the answer.**
**Relevance:** In recent times, Sher Shah Suri has been in the news for multiple reasons because the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) has undertaken restoration works at his mausoleum in Sasaram, Bihar, a UNESCO World Heritage tentative site.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the land revenue administration during the reign of Akbar:
I. - Qanungos were hereditary local officials and acted as custodians of revenue records.
II. 	 Each Karori was responsible for ensuring the collection of one crore dams in revenue.
III. 	 Amalguzar was in charge of assessment and collection of land revenue in the empire.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **land revenue system** formed the financial backbone of the Mughal Empire, particularly under **Emperor Akbar**, who introduced administrative and fiscal reforms that ensured both efficiency and fairness. Guided by his able finance minister, **Raja Todar Mal**, Akbar implemented a systematic revenue policy known as the **Dahsala system**. This system was based on accurate land measurement, classification, and price data collection.
● **Qanungos were hereditary officers**, generally belonging to influential local families, who acted as **custodians of revenue records** **and traditions** of their respective districts or parganas. During Akbar’s reign, they were tasked with providing factual data on agricultural productivity, soil condition, crop prices, and local customs related to land tenure. This ensured accurate assessment of revenue demands and reduced arbitrariness in taxation. Their hereditary nature ensured continuity, though Akbar later brought them under greater state supervision to curb corruption. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Karori system was introduced during Akbar’s reign (around 1580 CE) as part of an administrative experiment aimed at enhancing fiscal discipline. **Each Karori was responsible for ensuring the** **collection of one crore dams** (equivalent to roughly Rs. 2,50,000) in revenue. They verified information supplied by the qanungos and ensured that the official records matched the actual collection and cultivation data. This role combined fiscal responsibility with an auditing function, thereby improving accountability within the Mughal bureaucracy. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **The** **Amalguzar** **was a key revenue officer, directly responsible for both the assessment** **(****jama****) and the collection (****hasil****) of land revenue** at the district or sub-district level. He worked under the supervision of the Diwan and coordinated with qanungos, patwaris, and karoris to ensure the smooth functioning of the revenue administration. The Amalguzar thus acted as the operational link between the central revenue department and local officials, ensuring the efficient realisation of state revenue.  **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The **Dahsala system** (introduced by Raja Todar Mal in 1580 CE) assessed land revenue based on the average produce and prices of the past ten years, fixing **one-third of the average** **produce** as the state's share.
● **Qanungos** preserved local customs, revenue records, and boundaries, while **Patwaris** maintained village-level land data.
● **Karoris** combined the role of a collector and auditor, symbolising early efforts at administrative accountability.
● Akbar’s emphasis on land measurement (**zabt system**) and accurate record-keeping became the foundation of the Mughal agrarian system, influencing later colonial land revenue practices under the British.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText: TThe terms Bandobast, Zabti, and Dahsala in Mughal administration referred to:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● During the Mughal period, the land revenue system was the backbone of the empire’s economy and administration. It was under Emperor Akbar and his finance minister Raja Todar Mal that the system attained a remarkable level of organisation and efficiency. The methods known as **Bandobast, Zabti,** **and Dahsala** became key features of this system, ensuring regularity, fairness, and accurate assessment of agricultural produce. The terms Bandobast, Zabti, and Dahsala refer to various aspects of **land** **revenue assessment and collection** introduced during Akbar’s reign.
● **Bandobast** literally means “settlement” or “arrangement”. It referred to the **overall administrative** **process of land revenue assessment,** determining the produce, fixing the rate of tax, and maintaining records. It was the general term for organising and standardising the empire’s fiscal machinery.
● **Zabti** was the **method of actual measurement and assessment of land** for fixing revenue. Under this system, land was measured using standard units, classified according to fertility and productivity, and the average produce was calculated to determine the state’s share.
● **Dahsala** system, introduced by **Raja Todar Mal** in 1580 CE, was based on the **zabti** **principle.** It fixed land revenue on the basis of the **average produce and prices of the preceding ten years (dah =** **ten)**. The cultivator paid one-third of this average produce as revenue to the state, generally in cash. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText: With reference to the advent of Europeans in India, consider the following statements:

options.a: Share of the state in land revenue

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Agriculture was the primary source of state revenue in the Mughal Empire, and therefore, maintaining accurate information about land, crops, and productivity was vital for the functioning of the empire’s economy. Akbar, under the guidance of Raja Todar Mal, introduced a scientific and detailed classification of land to ensure fair revenue assessment. **The terms Polaj, Parati, Chahchar, and Banjar were** **used in Mughal records to categorize agricultural land based on its cultivation status and** **fertility**. This classification was part of the broader Zabt system, which laid the foundation for an efficient agrarian administration.
● **Polaj**: Land under **continuous and regular cultivation every year,** never left fallow; usually the most fertile.
● **Parati**: Land **left uncultivated temporarily** for recovery but cultivated after a short gap.
● **Chahchar**: Land **left fallow for several years** but still capable of being cultivated again.
● **Banjar**: Land that had been **uncultivated for a long period** and had become waste or unproductive.These classifications helped the revenue officers determine realistic tax assessments according to soil productivity and usage. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the advent of Europeans in India, consider the following statements:
I. - The Cartaz system was introduced in the Indian Ocean to control and tax maritime trade.
II. 	 In the Battle of Hooghly of 1759, the Dutch were decisively defeated in India.
III. 	 The Governor and Company of Merchants of London Trading into the East Indies was the first European trading company established in India.
IV. 	 In 1674, the first French Governor François Martin obtained a farman from the Sultan of Bijapur to set up a trading centre at Pondicherry.
How many of the above statements are not correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
European presence in India expanded through trading companies, naval dominance, and political interventions. The Portuguese introduced strict maritime control systems, while the English, Dutch, and French competed for commercial and territorial influence. Several key events and institutions shaped the trajectory of European expansion.
● **The Portuguese instituted the Cartaz system in the early 16th century to regulate Indian** **Ocean trade.** All merchant ships were required to obtain a pass (cartaz) from Portuguese authorities, enabling them to impose naval protection money and monopolise maritime commerce. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● **The Battle of Hooghly (1759) resulted in a decisive defeat of the Dutch by the English East** **India Company.** This battle ended Dutch ambitions in Bengal and established British naval and commercial supremacy in the region. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Although the English East India Company (chartered in 1600 as **“The Governor and Company of** **Merchants of London trading into the East Indies”**) was an early trading corporation, it was not the first European trading company in India. **Portuguese Estado da Índia (1505)** and Portuguese trading establishments predated the English by nearly a century. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● **In 1674, François Martin secured permission (farman) from Sher Khan Lodi, a local official** **under the Sultan of Bijapur, to establish a French settlement at Pondicherry.** This marked the foundation of French political and commercial presence in South India. **So, statement IV is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Regional States in the Medieval Period
questions.questionText:
Which of the following were the cultural contributions of the Deccan States?
I. - Miniature painting with romanticized aesthetic
II. 	 Dakhini Urdu a predecessor to North Indian Urdu
III. 	 Bidriware made of zinc alloy inlaid with silver and brass designs
IV. 	 Bulbous dome surrounded by a row of stone petals
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● During the Age of Akbar (16th century), while the Mughal Empire was consolidating its power in northern India, the Deccan Sultanate notably Bijapur and Golconda—emerged as prominent centres of art, architecture, and cultural synthesis. These states, though politically independent, shared cultural exchanges with the Mughal court.
● **Dakhini Urdu:** A significant development was the flourishing of Dakhini, a literary language that borrowed heavily from Arabic, Persian, Marathi, Kannada, and Telugu. It became a distinct predecessor to North Indian Urdu. **Sultan Muhammad Quli Qutb Shah** (r. 1580–1612) of the **Golconda** **Sultanate** as the most prominent Deccan king and poet who wrote in **Dakhini Urdu**. **So, point II is** **correct.**
● **Bidriware:** The distinctive metalwork art form of Bidriware, which features black zinc alloy inlaid with silver and brass designs, originated in the Bidar Sultanate. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Deccani Miniature Painting:** While the Mughal style (especially under Akbar and Jahangir) leaned toward realism, historical reportage, and precise naturalism, the Deccani school embraced a more lyrical, mystical, and romanticized aesthetic. **So, point I is correct.**
● **The "Deccani Dome":**
● **Bulbous Shape:** Unlike the semi-circular domes of Delhi, Deccani domes are often **bulbous** **or onion-shaped**.
● **Petal Base:** A signature feature is the base of the dome, which is often surrounded by a **row of** **stone petals** (looking like an open lotus), which masks the transition from the square drum to the circular dome. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
The **Deccan Sultanates—Ahmadnagar, Bijapur, Golconda, Bidar, and Berar**—served as cultural intermediaries between the Mughal North and the Vijayanagar South. They promoted unique architectural styles, such as the Indo-Persian domes, Dakhni Urdu poetry, and Deccani miniature painting. The Deccan school of painting, flourishing in the courts of Bijapur and Golconda, later influenced Mughal art during the reigns of Akbar and Jahangir.
**Relevance:** In recent years, the UNESCO World Heritage Centre and the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) have proposed several monuments from Bijapur (like Gol Gumbaz) and Hyderabad (like Charminar and Qutb Shahi Tombs) for inclusion in the UNESCO World Heritage List.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to Mughal period, consider the following statements:
I. - Khudkas were peasants who owned and tilled their own land.
II. 	 Taccavi was an agricultural loan to peasants in times of famine and drought.
III. 	 Talluqa was a revenue division under the control of a zamindar.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● During the **Mughal period**, the agrarian system formed the foundation of the empire’s economy and administration. Land revenue was the principal source of income for the state, and the structure of rural society was defined by intricate relationships between peasants, zamindars, and the state officials. Terms such as Khudkas, Taccavi, and Talluqa reflected different aspects of land ownership, cultivation, and administrative responsibility.
● **The term Khudkas (also called Khudkasht in some regions) referred to owner-cultivators** **who tilled the land they themselves possessed**. Unlike muzarian or tenant cultivators who worked on others’ land, Khudkas enjoyed a more stable position in the agrarian hierarchy, possessing hereditary rights over their holdings. They were responsible for paying land revenue directly to the state or its representatives. This class of peasants formed the backbone of rural production and ensured a steady revenue base for the administration. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Taccavi was a form of agricultural loan or advance granted by the state to peasants for the** **purposes of improving and expanding cultivation**. These loans were often given to help farmers purchase seeds, tools, or repair irrigation systems, especially after famines, floods, or other calamities. The policy continued under later regimes, including the British, where taccavi loans were formalised under revenue regulations. These advances reflected a measure of welfare within the Mughal agrarian policy aimed at maintaining agricultural productivity and social stability. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **The term Talluqa referred to a revenue division or jurisdictional unit under the control of** **a taluqdar or zamindar**. The zamindar or taluqdar had hereditary rights to collect land revenue from that area on behalf of the state, retaining a share as their remuneration. The talluqa was thus both an administrative and fiscal entity, forming part of the broader system of decentralised revenue collection. These hereditary intermediaries played a key role in linking the rural economy to the imperial treasury. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, only two of the given statements are correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The topic remains relevant today as India continues to address issues related to land ownership, tenancy, and agricultural credit.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
During the first half of the 17th century, the general
price level witnessed a significant rise in India.
**Statement II:**
The expansion of India’s foreign trade led to a
large inflow of gold and silver bullion.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above Statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II is the correct explanation
for Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II is **not** the correct explanation
for Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The 17th century marked a significant phase in India’s economic history during the Mughal Empire. The expansion of foreign trade, especially with European companies such as the Portuguese, Dutch, English, and French, reshaped the monetary and commercial landscape. India exported textiles, indigo, saltpetre, and spices, while receiving vast quantities of gold and silver in return.
● Historical economic studies—based on revenue records, European factory diaries, and Persian chronicles—indicate that **the price level in Mughal India experienced a steady rise in the early** **17th century and almost doubled**. This inflation was particularly visible in everyday commodities and agricultural produce. The major reason was the increase in the money supply, which circulated more widely due to expanding trade and monetisation of the economy. Mughal administrators, including those under Jahangir and Shah Jahan, recorded concerns about rising prices affecting revenue collection and urban life. **So, statement I is correct.**
● India enjoyed a favourable balance of trade due to high demand for its textiles and luxury goods in Europe and West Asia. Because European companies had little to offer India in exchange, they imported large quantities of gold and silver bullion to pay for Indian goods. **According to economic** historians such as Irfan Habib, India became one of the largest absorbers of world bullion in the 17th century. This bullion inflow increased the availability of money in circulation, contributing to monetisation and commercial expansion. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The influx of gold and silver increased the money supply in the Mughal economy, which contributed significantly to the rise in prices. This aligns with fundamental economic principles regarding money supply and inflation. **So,** both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II is the correct explanation for Statement I **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Rajasthan School of Painting, consider the following statements:
I. - The Mewar School illustrated literary works such as Rasikapriya and Bhagavata Purana.
II. 	 The Kishangarh School is known for the iconic portrayal of Bani-Thani.
III. 	 The Jaipur School absorbed Mughal artistic influences like naturalism and refined brushwork.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Rajasthan School of Painting, also known as the Rajput painting tradition, flourished between the 16th and 19th centuries across the princely states of Rajputana. These schools were deeply influenced by local traditions, regional histories, religious themes, and varying degrees of Mughal artistic influence.
● **The Mewar School** (centred in Chittor and later Udaipur) is one of the earliest and most vibrant branches of Rajput painting. It is distinguished by **strong, bold outlines, vivid and contrasting** **colours, and a direct, expressive style**. Themes commonly included the lives of local rulers, particularly Rana Pratap, courtly scenes, and religious narratives. The Mewar artists also illustrated literary works such as Rasikapriya and Bhagavata Purana. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **The Kishangarh School** reached its zenith under Raja Savant Singh (Nagari Das) and the master painter Nihal Chand. It is renowned for its graceful, elongated figures, sharp yet delicate facial features (especially the long nose and arched eyebrows), and a romantic, dream-like aesthetic. The most famous theme is the Radha–Krishna love tradition, including the **iconic portrayal of Bani-Thani,** often likened to the “Mona Lisa of India.” **So, statement II is correct.**
● **The Jaipur School, expanding under Sawai Jai Singh II, absorbed considerable Mughal** **artistic influence, especially in naturalism, refined brushwork**, and portraiture. Jaipur became an important centre for manuscript illustration, including astronomical texts, Ragamala series, and courtly records. Court portraits, hunting scenes, and detailed architectural depictions are characteristic features that reflect the combined Rajput–Mughal synthesis. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The Rajasthan School evolved into several sub-schools, including **Bundi, Kota, Marwar, and** **Dhola Maru traditions**, each with unique visual elements.
● Many Rajput courts commissioned illustrated manuscripts of the **Gita Govinda, Ramayana,** **and Ragamala**, reflecting devotional and courtly themes.
● The decline of Mughal patronage in the late 17th century pushed many artists to Rajput courts, enriching the regional styles.
● The pigments used were derived from natural sources—lapis lazuli, indigo, vermilion, gold leaf— showcasing sophisticated indigenous techniques.
**Relevance:** Rajasthan’s traditional arts have received renewed attention due to UNESCO heritage nominations, state-led initiatives to preserve miniature painting traditions, and increased global demand for traditional art forms.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Regional States in the Medieval Period
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Sikh Gurus and their contributions, consider the following statements:
I. - Guru Hargobind completed the compilation of the Sikh scripture the Adi Granth.
II. 	 Guru Gobind Singh founded the military brotherhood Khalsa in 1699.
III. 	 Guru Arjan Dev introduced the doctrine of Miri and Piri, signifying the integration of temporal and spiritual authority.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Sikh tradition evolved through the teachings and institutional contributions of ten Gurus, who shaped both the spiritual and socio-political identity of the community.
● **Guru Arjan Dev compiled the Adi Granth in 1604**, incorporating the hymns of the preceding Gurus and several Bhakti and Sufi saints. It was installed in the Harmandir Sahib and became the foundational scripture of Sikhism, forming the basis of the later Guru Granth Sahib. **So, statement I is** **not correct.**
● **Guru Gobind Singh established the Khalsa in 1699 at Anandpur Sahib** during the festival of Vaisakhi. The Khalsa created a distinct martial and ethical identity for Sikhs, marked by initiation rites and the adoption of the Five Ks, strengthening the community’s unity and defence. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● **Guru Hargobind introduced the concept of Miri-Piri, emphasising that a Sikh must uphold** **both spiritual devotion and temporal responsibility.** He wore two swords symbolising these principles and established the Akal Takht as the seat of temporal authority, marking the beginning of Sikh militarisation. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
Which of the following provisions were provided for in the Government of India Act, 1935?
I. - All-India Federation
II. 	 Provincial autonomy
III. 	 Dyarchy at the Centre
IV. 	 Abolition of communal electorates
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Government of India Act, 1935, was the most comprehensive constitutional reform introduced by the British Government prior to India’s independence. It laid the structural foundation for federalism, provincial autonomy, and judicial institutions in British India. Many of its features later influenced the Indian Constitution.
● **The Government of India Act, 1935, proposed the establishment of an All-India Federation** comprising British Indian provinces and princely states. However, this federation was never implemented because it required voluntary accession by the princely states, many of which did not consent. Still, the proposal itself was a major constitutional feature of the Act. **So, point I is correct.**
● **The Act introduced provincial autonomy**, replacing dyarchy at the provincial level. Elected provincial ministries were granted powers over subjects without interference from executive councillors appointed by the British. This marked a shift toward responsible government in provinces such as Madras, Bombay, and the United Provinces. **So, point II is correct.**
● The Act abolished dyarchy in the provinces but **introduced dyarchy at the Centre**. Federal subjects were divided into “reserved” and “transferred” categories, with the Governor-General retaining control over the reserved subjects. This central dyarchy was widely criticised and, like the federation, was never put into effect. **So, point III is correct.**
● **The Act of 1935 did not abolish communal electorates**. In fact, it continued and even expanded communal and special electorates to more categories, including women, labour, and business communities. Communal electorates had been introduced earlier under the 1909 Morley–Minto Reforms and continued through the Montagu–Chelmsford Reforms (1919). **So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to Shivaji’s administration,
consider the following statements:
I. - Shivaji organised his central administration
based on the Ashta Pradhan system.
II. 	 Chauth and Sardeshmukhi were regular state
taxes collected from peasants in the kingdom.
III. 	 Shivaji discouraged the use of artillery
in forts due to mobility challenges in the
Sahyadri terrain.
IV. 	 Administrative offices were assigned purely
based on merit and loyalty.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Shivaji’s administration was noted for its efficiency, discipline, and strong central control. He built a system that balanced civil administration, military structure, and revenue management while reducing the dominance of hereditary elites. His reforms laid the foundations of the Maratha state and contributed to its later expansion.
● **Shivaji established the Ashta Pradhan Mandal**, comprising eight ministers such as the Peshwa, Amatya, Sachiv, and Senapati, each responsible for specific administrative and military functions. Though advisory in nature, the council played a central role in governance. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Shivaji institutionalised Chauth (25% of revenue) and Sardeshmukhi (an additional 10%) as** **levies imposed on neighbouring territories,** particularly Mughal and Deccan regions, in return for protection. These became major sources of Maratha income. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Shivaji did not discourage the use of artillery; rather, he maintained light and mobile** **artillery suited for hill forts and guerrilla warfare.** His forts like Raigad and Pratapgad were equipped with cannons adapted to the mountainous terrain. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● **Shivaji discouraged hereditary claims to offices and ensured administrative positions were** **based on merit and loyalty.** This prevented the rise of entrenched local power centres and strengthened the authority of the central state. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of Land Purpose
I. - Khalisa
Income from these
villages went directly
to the royal exchequer
II. - Inam lands Allotted to learned and
religious men
III. - Jagirs
Allotted to nobles and
members of the royal
family including the
queens
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Mughal Empire, like many other medieval Indian polities, derived its strength from a well-organized land revenue system. Land not only served as the principal source of state income but also as an instrument of political control and reward. The Mughals classified land into various categories such as Khalisa, Jagir, and Inam, each serving a distinct administrative or political purpose.
● **Khalisa land referred to the territory whose revenues were reserved exclusively for the** **imperial treasury**. These lands were under direct control of the central administration and were not assigned as jagirs or inam grants. Revenue collected from khalisa villages financed royal expenditures such as the maintenance of the army, the palace, and other state establishments. **So, pair I is correctly** **matched.**
● **Inam (meaning “reward” or “gift”) lands were granted to scholars, religious leaders, poets,** **and pious individuals in recognition of their learning or service**. These grants were generally tax-free and hereditary, though subject to royal confirmation. Such grants strengthened the moral and cultural legitimacy of the Mughal rule and were also meant to encourage learning and the propagation of religion. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Jagirs were land assignments given to nobles, officers, and members of the royal family** in lieu of salaries or as rewards for service to the empire. The holders of these lands, known as Jagirdars, collected revenue from the territory to maintain their troops and meet administrative expenses. While the ownership of the land remained with the state, the Jagirdar enjoyed the right to collect revenue. **So,** **pair III is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Several states, including Telangana and Maharashtra, have been reviewing old Inam and Jagir records to regularise ownership and resolve disputes.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the ideological positions of Mahatma Gandhi and Dr B.R. Ambedkar, consider the following statements:
I. - While Gandhi sought moral transformation within Hindu society, Ambedkar aimed at structural annihilation of caste to bring social equality.
II. 	 Both Gandhi and Ambedkar supported protective political safeguards for the Depressed Classes.
III. 	 Unlike Ambedkar, Gandhi rejected the idea of separate electorates for the Depressed Classes.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The ideological relationship between Mahatma Gandhi and Dr B.R. Ambedkar constitutes one of the most significant debates in India’s modern political and social history. Both leaders shared a commitment to improving the conditions of the Depressed Classes (later Scheduled Castes), but they sharply disagreed on the methods needed to achieve social equality. Their differences spanned issues such as caste reform, representation, and the nature of social justice.
● **Gandhi believed** in the reform of Hindu society from within and held that caste, though distorted by untouchability, **could be morally purified through spiritual reform** and social conscience. He saw untouchability as a moral sin but did not advocate abolishing the varna framework entirely. Ambedkar, in contrast, argued that the **caste system itself was inherently oppressive and irredeemable**, and that **meaningful equality required its complete structural destruction**. His work, **The** **Annihilation of Caste**, clearly underscores that no reform within Hindu society could sufficiently eradicate caste-based discrimination. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Both leaders agreed on the need for political safeguards, but Ambedkar preferred separate** **electorates** to ensure an autonomous political voice for the Depressed Classes. Gandhi opposed separate electorates on the grounds that they would fragment Hindu society and further isolate the oppressed. **Gandhi instead supported joint electorates with reservations**, as later reflected in the Poona Pact. Thus, although their goals of upliftment aligned, their strategies diverged significantly. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Gandhi’s opposition to separate electorates** was strong enough to lead him to undertake a fast unto death in Yerawada Jail in 1932. **Ambedkar, however, believed that only separate** **electorates** could empower Depressed Classes to choose their own leaders without pressure from dominant castes. This position formed the core of his negotiations before the Poona Pact. He later accepted reserved seats within joint electorates only after intense political and moral pressure. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
1. Gandhi and Ambedkar’s disagreements were not personal but stemmed from
fundamentally different philosophical frameworks: Gandhi’s ethical–spiritual reformism versus Ambedkar’s constitutional–structural transformation.
2. Ambedkar later became the Chairman of the Drafting Committee of the Constitution,
embedding his principles of equality, non-discrimination, and affirmative action into the constitutional framework.
3. Gandhi emphasised Harijan upliftment through constructive programmes, whereas
Ambedkar emphasised legal rights, representation, and state intervention.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: India on the eve of British Conquest
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs with reference to rise of regional states with decline of Mughal power in India:
Types of states Examples
I. - Successor states Awadh, Bengal
and Hyderabad
II. - Independent
kingdoms
Maratha and the
Sikh
III. - New states Mysore and the
Rajput
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
By 1761 the Mughal empire was empire only in name, as its weaknesses had enabled the local powers to assert their independence.
● **Bengal, Hyderabad and Awadh** were the three successor states of the Mughal empire, in the sense that they were founded by Mughal provincial governors who never formally severed their links with the centre, but virtually exercised autonomy in matters of execution of power at the local level. **So, pair I** **is correctly matched.**
o The **province or the subah of Bengal** gradually became independent of Mughal control after
**Murshid Quli Khan became the governor in 1717**. o The autonomous **kingdom of Hyderabad was founded in 1724** by a powerful noble at the
imperial court, **Chin Qulich Khan**, who eventually took the title of **Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah** **I**. o **Saadat Khan was appointed the Mughal governor of Awadh in 1722** with the difficult
charge of subduing rebellions by the local rajas and chiefs. Frustrated in court politics, **Saadat** **then decided to build up a power base in Awadh** and as a first step had his son-in-law Safdar Jung recognised by the emperor as his deputy governor.
● **Independent Kingdoms** came into existence primarily due to the destabilisation of the Mughal control over the provinces, examples being **Mysore and the Rajput states**. Marathas rose to power challenging Mughal authority in the Deccan. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **New states** are states founded by rebels against the Mughal state, such as the **Marathas, the Sikhs,** **the Jats and the Afghan kingdoms** of **Farukhabad** and **Rohilkhand**.
o The Kingdom of Mysore was founded in 1399 AD by Yaduraya Wodeyar as a vassal state of
Vijaynagar empire. The kingdom became independent after the Battle of Talikota (1565). **So, pair** **III is not correctly matched.** **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore**, **option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: India on the eve of British Conquest
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements best describes the role of Chatuspathis, functioning in the 18th century?

options.a: They functioned as village-level schools providing elementary instruction in vernacular languages.
options.b: They were centres of higher learning that imparted advanced education in Sanskrit texts, logic, and philosophy.
options.c: They were missionary institutions in rural areas established by the East India Company to promote English education.
options.d: They were technical institutions created to train Indians for administrative service under Company rule.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Chatuspathis or Tols, as they were called in Bihar and Bengal, were the centres of higher** **education.** The education imparted in 18th-century India was still traditional which could not match with the rapid developments in the West.
● They gave knowledge that was confined to literature, law, religion, philosophy, and logic and excluded the study of physical and natural sciences, technology, and geography.
● These were often supported by endowments from wealthy zamindars, rajas, or merchants. The teachers (Pandits) were highly respected. **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText: With reference to the history of India, Kanchan Nambiar, Tayumanavar, and Shah Abdul Latif were:

options.b: Poets who wrote in regional languages
options.c: Veterans in the Carnatic music tradition

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● While the 18th century was a period of political chaos (due to the fall of the Mughal Empire), culturally, it contributed to the growth of regional literature and arts. In South India, Malayalam literature flourished under the patronage of the Travancore rulers.
o **Kalakkathu Kunchan Nambiar was a noted Malayalam poet**. He invented the dance-art
form Ottan Thullal, which was created as a protest against the socio-political structure of the time. o **Tayumanavar**, one of the best exponents of **sittar poetry (in Tamil language)**, protested
the abuses of temple rule and the caste system. His poems (hymns) bridged the gap between the Saiva Siddhanta philosophy and Vedanta. o **Shah Abdul Latif** was a **Sufi poet of the Sindhi language**. His collected works are known
as "**Shah Jo Risalo**. **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Expansion and Consolidation of British Power
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the British conquest of Bengal:
I. - The Imperial Farman of 1717 exempted from duty the private trade of the Company’s officials.
II. 	 Mir Qasim shifted his capital from Calcutta to Murshidabad to distance his administration from the intrigues of the Company at Calcutta.
III. 	 The Diwani rights to the Company contributed to the drain of resources from Bengal.
Which of the statements given above are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Imperial Farman of 1717 (by Mughal Emperor Farrukhsiyar)** granted the East India Company the freedom of trade in Bengal without paying taxes, based on an annual payment of Rupees 3,000. **This privilege covered only the Company’s official trade.** It did not extend to the private trade of the Company’s officials. Company servants frequently misused the **dastaks (trade permits)** for their private trade to evade taxes, which caused huge revenue losses to the Nawab of Bengal and was a primary cause of conflict leading to the Battle of Plassey (1757). **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Mir Jafar** was removed from the throne to be **replaced by his son-in-law Mir Kasim in October** **1760**. But conflict arose again over the misuse of trade privileges by the Company’s servants. Unable to stop the misuse of dastaks, **Mir Qasim** **abolished internal duties altogether,** so that the Indian merchants could also enjoy the same privilege.
o **Mir Qasim shifted the capital from Murshidabad to Munger in Bihar.** Mir Qasim made
the move to distance himself from the British East India Company's excessive interference and control in Calcutta and Murshidabad and to rule more independently. . **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● **Diwani right of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa** was granted to the East India Company in lieu of an annual payment of Rs 26 lakh u**nder the Treaty of Allahabad (1765) by Shah Alam II**.
o Before acquiring the Diwani (1765), the British had to import bullion (gold/silver) from Europe
to pay for Indian textiles and spices. Silver imports by European companies, particularly the British, stopped almost completely once the East India Company got the right to collect the revenue of Bengal in 1765. This meant **Indian goods were being bought with Indian money** **and exported to Britain. India received no return (no bullion import) for these** **exports. So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore**, **option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Expansion and Consolidation of British Power
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements about the conquest of Punjab state by British East India Company:
I. - The Treaty of Amritsar (1809) fixed the Sutlej River as the boundary between Maharaja Ranjit Singh and the British.
II. 	 The Treaty of Lahore (1846) led to the total annexation of Punjab.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Treaty of Amritsar (1809)** was signed between Maharaja Ranjit Singh and the British (represented by Charles Metcalfe). The **treaty fixed the Sutlej River as the boundary between the two** **powers.** The treaty prevented Ranjit Singh from expanding East/South **of the Sutlej**, into the Cis-Sutlej states like Patiala and Nabha, which came under British protection. It gave him a free hand to expand to the West and North. Consequently, he conquered Multan, Kashmir, and Peshawar after this treaty. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The **Treaty of Lahore (1846) was signed after the First Anglo-Sikh War** (1845–46), not the Second. This treaty forced the Sikhs to cede the Jalandhar Doab (territory between the Beas and Sutlej rivers) and pay a heavy war indemnity (1.5 Crore). The **Second Anglo-Sikh War (1848–49)** led to the total annexation of Punjab. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore**, **option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The painting of the 19th century founder of the Sikh Empire, depicting Maharaja Ranjit Singh set an auction record in the UK.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Expansion and Consolidation of British Power
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the British Empire in India:
I. - The Treaty of Yandabo brought the province of Arakan under the control of the British.
II. 	 The ‘forward policy’ was adopted to protect the British India frontiers from Napoleon.
III. 	 The Treaty of Sugauli defined the boundary between Nepal and British India.
IV. 	 The Younghusband Expedition resulted in the formal annexation of Tibet into British India.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **First Anglo-Burmese War (1824–1826)** ended with the **Treaty of Yandabo (1826)**. Under the provisions of this treaty the King of Burma renounced claims on Assam, Cachar, and Jaintia and **ceded the coastal provinces of Arakan and Tenasserim to the British**. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **Auckland** who came to India as the governor general in 1836, **advocated a ‘forward policy’.** This implied that the Company government in India itself had to take initiatives to protect the boundary of British India **from a probable Russian attack**. This objective was to be achieved either through treaties with the neighbouring countries or by annexing them completely. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
o The **fear of a French invasion was a major concern during Lord Wellesley's time** (1798
to 1805). He adopted the policy of the Subsidiary Alliance.
● In 1801, the English annexed Gorakhpur**,** which brought the Gorkhas’ boundary and the Company’s boundary together. The conflict started due to the Gorkhas’ capture of Butwal and Sheoraj in the **period** **of Lord Hastings (1813–23)**.
o The **Anglo-Nepalese War (1814–1816)** ended with the **Treaty of Sugauli (1816)**. Nepal
ceded the districts of Garhwal and Kumaon and abandoned claims to the Terai region. **The** **treaty used two major rivers to define the new eastern and western limits of the** **Kingdom of Nepal.** o The **Kali River** was designated as the western border. Nepal had to renounce all claims to
territories lying to the west of this river. The **Mechi River** was established as the eastern border. Nepal had to cede all territories east of this river (including parts of Sikkim). **So, statement III** **is correct.**
● The **Younghusband Expedition to Tibet (1904)** sent by Lord Curzon to oblige the Tibetans to come to an agreement, to counter perceived Russian influence in Lhasa. The Tibetans refused to negotiate and offered non-violent resistance. Younghusband pushed his way into Lhasa (August 1904) while the Dalai Lama fled. The expedition forced the **Treaty of Lhasa (1904).** Tibet agreed to pay a large indemnity and open trade marts (Yatung, Gyantse, Gartok). **However, Tibet was not annexed**. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, Nepal objected to the China-India agreement on making Lipulekh a trade route.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Extremely localised nature of rebellion
II. 	 Lack of discipline and order among the rebels
III. 	 Act for the Better Government of India
IV. 	 Military inferiority of rebels
V. 	 Lack of communal unity among people
How many of the above given factors were responsible for the failure of the Revolt of 1857?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **revolt of 1857 was not an all-India phenomenon**. It was primarily **concentrated in the** **Northern and Central parts of India.** The eastern, southern, and western parts of India remained unaffected. This allowed the British to move troops from these quiet areas to crush the rebellion in the North without fear of losing control elsewhere. **So, point I is correct.**
● While the **sepoys** were individually brave, they **lacked a central command structure** and coordinated military strategy. The rebels often operated as unorganized mobs or independent units without coordination between leaders, **for example**, Nana Saheb in Kanpur and Begum Hazrat Mahal in Lucknow fought largely separate battles. **So, point II is correct.**
● **The "Act for the Better Government of India"** (Government of India Act, 1858) was a **consequence of the revolt.** The Government of India Act, passed by Parliament on 2 August 1858, transferred all Company’s powers to the Crown; declared Queen Victoria to be the sovereign of British India; and provided for the appointment of a Secretary of State. **So, point III is not correct.**
● The **British possessed superior military technology** (Enfield rifles), a vast naval force, and a **sophisticated communication network** (the electric telegraph). The rebels often fought with outdated weapons like swords, spears, and old muskets. The British telegraph allowed the commanderin-chief to track rebel movements and deploy troops faster than the rebels could march. **So, point IV** **is correct.**
● The **revolt of 1857 is celebrated for the high degree of Hindu-Muslim unity**. Soldiers of both communities fought side by side. The rebels unanimously proclaimed Bahadur Shah Zafar as Emperor. Cow slaughter was immediately banned in rebel-held areas to respect Hindu sentiments. **So, point V is** **not correct.** **So, only three of the above factors were responsible for the failure of the revolt of 1857.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Few Unsung Heroes of the 1857 Revolt**
● **Uda Devi (Lucknow):** A Dalit woman from the Pasi community, she formed a women’s battalion under Begum Hazrat Mahal. During the Battle of Sikandar Bagh, she climbed a pipal tree armed with a rifle and ammunition. She acted as a sniper, killing over 30 British soldiers before being spotted and shot dead.
● **Jhalkari Bai – The Queen's Shadow (Jhansi)**: She was a soldier in Rani Lakshmibai's women's army (Durga Dal). She bore a striking physical resemblance to the Queen.
o During the siege of Jhansi, she disguised herself as Rani Lakshmibai and rode
out to command the army.
● **Peer Ali Khan (Patna):** A humble bookbinder who used his shop as a clandestine meeting point for rebels and secretly distributed pamphlets and coded messages to organize the uprising in Bihar.
● **Rani Avantibai Lodhi**, the queen of Ramgarh (**Madhya Pradesh**): To unite the wavering kings and chieftains of the neighboring regions, she sent them glass bangles with a letter stating: "If you cannot fight for your motherland, wear these bangles and sit at home." This shamed many fence-sitters into joining the revolt.
**Relevance**: Defence Minister Rajnath Singh hailed Dalit resistance fighter Uda Devi Pasi for her courage and defiance against British rule in the 1857 revolt.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Young Bengal movement:
I. - It was inspired by the European radical youth groups.
II. 	 Its leader was Louis Henri Derozio who organized the Academic Association in 1828.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Henry Louis Vivian Derozio**, a teacher at Hindu College, Calcutta, in the 1820s, promoted radical ideas and encouraged his pupils to question all authority. **Referred to as the Young Bengal** **Movement,** his students attacked tradition and custom, demanded education for women and campaigned for the freedom of thought and expression.
o The Young Bengal Movement was directly inspired by **European radical youth groups** like
Young Italy (led by Giuseppe Mazzini) and Young Ireland, adopting their spirit of idealistic nationalism, free thought, and calls for social/political reform. Although they applied these ideas to the Indian context under Henry Derozio's guidance, promoting rationalism and challenging colonial rule and tradition. . **So, statement I is correct.**
● He encouraged his students (the "Derozians") to question all authority, love truth, and think for themselves. **He organized the Academic Association in 1828**, which became a forum for debating subjects like free will, fate, and virtue. **So, statement II is correct.**
o They posed an intellectual challenge to the religious and social orthodoxy of Hinduism. It was
they who **formed in 1838 the ‘Society for the Acquisition of General Knowledge’,** where they discussed various aspects of Western science, and stood for several social reforms, such as the prohibition of caste taboos, child marriage, kulin polygamy or the ban on widow remarriage. **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements about Dayananda Saraswati:
I. - He rejected the authority of the Puranas and regarded the Vedas as infallible.
II. 	 He believed in the Vedic Chaturvarna system and that one’s Varna should be determined by birth.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Swami Dayanand** **Saraswati** was born on February 12, 1824, in Gujarat. Moola Shankar was Dayanand's original name. The philosophy of Dayananda Saraswati can be known from his three famous contributions namely “Satyartha Prakash”, “Veda Bhashya Bhumika” and “Veda Bhashya Bhumika”. **Dayanand Saraswati strongly rejected the authority of the Puranas**, viewing them as corrupted, post-Vedic additions responsible for many social and religious "evils" in Hinduism. **Swami Dayananda’s** **central slogan was "Go Back to the Vedas.**" He believed that the **Vedas were the infallible** **word of God** and the source of all true knowledge. In 1875 he founded Arya Samaj. **So, statement I** **is correct.**
● In the Haryana–Punjab region, **where** **karewa** **(levirate marriage)**, in which the widow was accepted as the bride by one of the brothers of the deceased husband, was a common practice among most of the landowning castes.
o Karewa emanated from the need to retain landed property within the patrilineal family. **K****arewa**
**was advocated by the Arya Samajists** from the 1880s even though **Swami Dayanand** **Saraswati**, was **not in favour of widow remarriage**.
● **Swami Dayananda** **Saraswati** **criticized the hereditary caste system** based on birth. He **believed in the Vedic Chaturvarna system** but argued that one’s Varna should be determined by Guna (character), Karma (action), and Swabhav (nature), not by birth.
o He argued that a Shudra who learned the Vedas and lived a noble life should be treated as a
Brahmin, and a Brahmin who acted immorally should be treated as a Shudra. This was a radical call for an **open social system**. o In his open social system women and shudras received a measure of learning and made education
and not birth the determinant of status. He denounced the worship of gods and goddesses and advocated the worship of the Supreme Being. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore**, **option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Veda Samaj**
● Established in Madras (Chennai) in 1864, the Veda Samaj was inspired by the Brahmo Samaj.
● It worked to abolish caste distinctions and promote widow remarriage and women’s education. Its members believed in one God.
● They condemned the superstitions and rituals of orthodox Hinduism.
**Relevance**: The Prime Minister recently hailed Arya Samaj’s 150-year legacy and calls it a celebration of India’s Vedic identity at International Arya Mahasammelan 2025.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Foundation of INC and Moderate Phase
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Advocated self-reliance
II. 	 Indianization of civil services
III. 	 Mass mobilization as a strategy
IV. 	 Use of cultural nationalism
How many of the above strategies were adopted by Moderates in the Indian National Congress?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The national leaders like Dadabhai Naoroji, Pherozeshah Mehta, D.E. Wacha, W.C. Bonnerjea, S.N. Banerjea who dominated the Congress policies during the early period (1885–1905) were staunch believers in ‘liberalism’ and ‘moderate’ politics and came to be labelled as Moderates.
● The **Moderates believed in the "3 Ps"**—Prayer, Petition, and Protest. They had full faith in the British sense of justice and worked within the constitutional framework.
o It was the Extremists who dismissively called this approach "**Political** **Mendicancy**" (begging)
and instead advocated for Atma Shakti (Self-Reliance). **So, point I is not correct.**
● **Moderates (like Dadabhai Naoroji, R.C. Dutt)** formulated the **Economic Critique of British** **Imperialism (Drain Theory).** They argued that the British bureaucracy in India was un-British, expensive, and exploitative, even if they believed the British Crown/Parliament was inherently just. They campaigned for the **"Indianization of services"** to break this bureaucratic monopoly and this demand was agreed gradually. **So, point II is correct.**
● The **Moderates** were **elitist** and feared that mass **mobilization would lead to lawlessness and** **chaos.** They stuck to intellectual agitation. **The Extremists introduced the concept of Passive** **Resistance** (refusal to cooperate with the government) and brought the masses into the Swadeshi movement. **So, point III is not correct.**
● The **Moderates were often Western-educated intellectuals** who admired Western political institutions and liberalism. **The Extremists used cultural nationalism** to revive self-pride. **Tilak** **started the Ganpati and Shivaji festivals**, and **Aurobindo/Bankim Chandra used Hindu** **imagery (Mother Goddess)** to counter the colonial narrative of the "White Man's Burden" (civilizing mission). **So, point IV is not correct.** **So, only one of the above methods were used by Moderates in the freedom struggle.** **Therefore**, **option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the ‘Swadeshi Movement’,
consider the following:
I. - Bonfires of foreign clothes and boycott of
British educational institutions
II. 	 Samitis were established to spread political
consciousness
III. 	 Raksha bandhan was observed as a symbol
of brotherhood
IV. 	 Promotion of indigenous education and
Industries
How many of the above methods were adopted to
protest against the Partition of Bengal?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Curzon’s (1899–1905) scheme to partition Bengal, instead of weakening Bengali nationalists who, he believed, controlled the Congress, revitalized it in unexpected ways. In retaliation for the Partition of Bengal, the **swadeshi movement was launched**. The formal proclamation of the Swadeshi Movement was made on **August 7, 1905,** at the Calcutta Town Hall and Boycott Resolution was passed. Boycott spread to other parts of India, particularly Punjab, Maharashtra and the Telugu-speaking areas of the Madras Presidency, in solidarity with Bengal.
● **Huge heaps of foreign clothes were burnt at crossroads.** This was not just economic but symbolic of severing ties with the British. Between 1905 and 1906, there was a 22 per cent fall in the quantity of imported cotton piece goods, 44 per cent in cotton twist and yarn, 11 per cent in salt, 55 per cent in cigarettes and 68 per cent in boots and shoes.
o When the British government issued the **Carlyle Circular (October 1905)** threatening to
withdraw grants from schools whose students participated in protests, the leaders called for a **boycott of these schools**. **So, point I is correct.**
● Samitis such as the **Swadesh Bandhab Samiti of Ashwini Kumar Dutta** (in Barisal) emerged as a very popular and powerful means of mass mobilisation. In Tirunelveli, Tamil Nadu, **V.O. Chidambaram** **Pillai, Subramania Siva**, and some lawyers formed the **Swadeshi Sangam** which inspired the local masses.
o These samitis generated political consciousness among the masses through magic lantern lectures,
swadeshi songs, providing physical and moral training to their members. **So, point II is correct.**
● **On the appeal made by Rabindranath Tagore and Ramendrasundar Trivedi**, secretary of the Bangiya Sahitya Parishad (Literary Council of Bengal, founded in 1894 and headed by Trivedi between 1904 and 1911), **on 16 October 1905, the day when the partition came into effect,** **rakhi** **(rakhsha) bandhan** was observed, **wristlets of coloured thread were exchanged** as a symbol of brotherhood and unity. **So, point III is correct.**
● The **movement emphasized Atma-Shakti (Self-Reliance)**. British crackdown on students and the government’s threat to withdraw grants from nationalist dominated institutions gave force to the movement for the boycott of British educational institutions and the founding of national schools.
o This effort was bolstered by the handsome contribution of 100,000 by Raja Subodh Mullick. **A**
**National Council of Education was set up in 1906**, and the **Bengal National College** **and Bengal Technical Institute** were established. o **Acharya P.C. Ray** established the Bengal Chemicals and Pharmaceutical Works (the first Indian
pharmaceutical company). o In Madras, V.O. Chidambaram Pillai set up the Swadeshi Steam Navigation Company to challenge
the British monopoly on shipping. **So, point IV is correct.** **So, all the above methods were adopted to protest against the Partition of Bengal.** **Therefore**, **option (d) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Amid Trump’s tariff tirade, Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s Swadeshi call harks back to the Swadeshi movement of 1905-1911.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Introduction of non-official majority in the
provincial legislative councils
II. 	 Members of the legislative councils were
allowed to move resolutions on the budget
III. 	 Direct election was introduced for the
provincial legislatures
IV. 	 Indians were to be appointed to the viceroy’s
executive council
How many of the above reforms were introduced
by the Morley-Minto Reforms of 1909?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Indian Councils Act of 1909, known as the Morley–Minto Reform**s (after the Secretary of State Lord Morley and the Viceroy, the Earl of Minto), came as a concession after the intense repression leashed on swadeshi activists—extremists and revolutionary terrorists.
● The Councils Act of 1909 **provided for limited self-government by increasing the number of** **Indians,** while the British maintained an Official Majority in the Central Legislative Council to ensure control, **they allowed a Non-Official Majority in the Provincial Legislative Councils.** But many of the ‘non-official’ members were to be nominated, not elected. **So, point I is correct**
● It significantly expanded the deliberative functions of the legislative councils, granting members the power to **move resolutions on the budget** and on matters of **general public interest**.
o Members were empowered to move resolutions related to the budget, suggest alterations in
taxation, or propose new loans. While they could **debate and vote on separate items** within the budget, they did not have the power to vote on the budget as a whole. o In addition to moving resolutions, members were granted the right to ask **supplementary**
**questions** to seek further information from the executive. o Resolutions passed by the councils were primarily **recommendatory** and not legally binding on
the government. **So, point II is correct**.
● The **system of election introduced was Indirect**, not direct. Local Bodies (municipalities/district boards) elected an Electoral College. The Electoral College elected members to the Provincial Councils. Provincial Councils elected members to the Central Council.
o Elections, moreover, were constrained by several qualifications, income being an important one.
Muslims with a certain income were entitled to vote while Hindus with the same income were deemed too poor to qualify. Finally, the Government of India reserved the power to disqualify any candidate from contesting the elections, if he was considered politically dangerous. **So, point** **III is not correct.**
● The Act provided for the **appointment of an Indian** to the Viceroy’s Executive Council (Law Member). Satyendra Prasanna Sinha (S.P. Sinha) became the first Indian to join the Viceroy’s Executive Council. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Other Feature** o The system of **Separate Electorates** was first introduced in India by the Indian Councils Act
of 1909. Under this system, Muslim members were to be elected only by Muslim voters. A Hindu or a Christian living in that constituency could not vote for the Muslim representative. **So, only two of the above reforms were introduced by Morley-Minto Reforms of 1909.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The demand for Home Rule intensified during World War I.
**Statement II:** The wartime economic hardships like inflation and increased taxation created widespread public dissatisfaction. **Statement III:** The Home Rule Leagues sought to impress upon the British the necessity of granting selfgovernment in India.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct, but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Annie Besant (1847–1933)** became the head of the Theosophical Society based in Adyar, Madras, and used this core group as the base to popularize the militant ideas of the Irish Home Rule League, particularly in Madras and Bombay, and in parts of the United Provinces.
o **The Home Rule Movement was the Indian response to the First World War** in a less
charged but more effective way than the response of Indians living abroad which took the form of the romantic Ghadr adventure. The war, being fought among the major imperialist powers of the day and backed by naked propaganda against each other, exposed the myth of white superiority. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **World War I** led to a massive drain on India's resources. **Prices** of essential commodities **skyrocketed**. **Taxes were increased** to fund the war effort. This economic **misery turned the** **common man against the government**. The Home Rule Leagues successfully channeled this economic discontent into a political demand, thereby intensifying the movement. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● The **Home Rule Leagues were not revolutionary or secessionist movements.** They did not demand total independence (Purna Swaraj). They **used constitutional methods** (meetings, lectures, pamphlets) to "impress upon" the British the need for reform.
o The aim was to be achieved by promoting political education and discussion through public
meetings, organising libraries and reading rooms containing books on national politics, holding conferences, organising classes for students on politics, carrying out propaganda through newspapers. **So, statement III is correct.** o However, this statement does not describe the reason behind intensification of the Home Rule
League Movement during world war I. **So, both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Young Men’s Indian Association:**
● The YMIA was **founded by Dr. Annie Besant** on 15.3.1914, as a trust to promote cultural, social, and political activities of Indian youth.
● Its aim was to build youth’s bodies, minds, and character to lead the nation.
● Its functions included promoting physical activities, fostering intellectual growth through debates and lectures, and cultivating patriotic and moral development to prepare the youth for leadership and national service. **Swarajya Sabha:**
● In April 1920, after Annie Besant resigned, **Mahatma Gandhi was elected as the** **President of the All-India Home Rule League.**
● In October 1920, Gandhi formally changed the name of the "All India Home Rule League" to the Swarajya Sabha.
**Relevance**: The Hindu newspaper recently reported about the dismal condition of Chennai's Gokhale Hall, once the heart of the Home Rule movement.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Emergence of Gandhi and Early Movements
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Satyagraha seeks to win over the wrongdoer rather than to defeat or humiliate them.
**Statement II:** Satyagraha believes that every human being possesses an inner conscience that can be awakened through truth and compassion.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and statement II are correct, but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct, but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect, but statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Satyagraha (Truth-force or Soul-force)** is fundamentally different from "Passive Resistance." In passive resistance, the aim might be to harass or embarrass the opponent into submission. In Satyagraha, **the goal is never to coerce, defeat, or humiliate the opponent. Instead, the goal is to bring** **about a "Change of Heart.**" The Satyagrahi appeals to the opponent's reason and conscience, making them realize the injustice they are committing. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Gandhi’s entire philosophy rested on the conviction that human nature is essentially good.** He believed that every person, no matter how tyrannical, possesses a spark of the divine or an inner conscience. This conscience may be dormant or clouded by ignorance/greed, but it is never destroyed. It can be awakened not by brute force (which provokes a defensive reaction) but by self-suffering (Tapasya) and Compassion (Ahimsa). **So, statement II is correct.** **So, both Statement–II and Statement–II are correct and statement I explains Statement–I.** **Therefore**, **option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Satyagraha Sabha**
● **The Satyagraha Sabha was founded by Mahatma Gandhi in Bombay in February** **1919.**
● The Satyagraha Sabha organized the famous **"Rowlatt Satyagraha"** on April 6, 1919, against the Rowlatt Act (The Anarchical and Revolutionary Crimes Act, 1919).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText: The Montagu–Chelmsford Reforms of 1919 introduced which of the following reforms?

options.a: It set up a bicameral system at the provincial level.
options.b: Elected Indian ministers were made responsible to the central legislature.
options.c: Revenue resources were divided between the center and the provinces.
options.d: Communal representation introduced in the Morley-Minto reforms was abolished.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms, enacted via the Government of India Act 1919, introduced several major changes to the governance of British India.
● **Revenue resources were divided:** For the first time, **the Act separated the provincial budget** **from the central budget.** It demarcated specific sources of revenue, assigning land revenue to the provinces and keeping income tax with the central government.
● **Bicameral system at the central level: The Act introduced bicameralism at the central level** **(the Council of State and the Legislative Assembly), not the provincial level.** Provincial legislatures remained unicameral, though they were expanded to have an elected majority (at least 70%).
● **Control over 'transferred' subjects:**  While elected Indian ministers in the provinces were in charge of 'transferred' subjects, they were responsible to the provincial legislative councils, not the imperial (central) legislature. **At the central level, the executive remained entirely irresponsible to the** **legislature.**
● **Communal representation: Rather than being abolished, communal representation was** **extended.** In addition to Muslims, separate electorates were granted to Sikhs, Anglo-Indians, Indian Christians, and Europeans. **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Revolutionary Activity
During the 1920s, consider the following
information:
Revolutionary
group Key Leader Major
activity
I.
Hindustan
Republican
Association
Ram Prasad
Bismil
Kakori
Train
Robbery
II. - Yugantar
Group
Sachindra
Nath Sanyal
Chittagong
armoury
raid
III. - Kirti Kisan
Party Surya Sen
Propagating
socialist
ideology
among
peasants
and workers
In how many of the above rows is the given
information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Revolutionary activity in the 1920s represented a critical phase of India’s anti-colonial struggle. After the decline of the Non-Cooperation Movement (1922), disillusioned youth turned to reorganised revolutionary groups in North India, Punjab, and Bengal. These groups—revitalised by new ideological influences such as socialism— attempted to revive armed resistance, propagate radical ideas, and build disciplined organisations for national liberation.
● **The Hindustan Republican Association (HRA) was founded in 1924 by Sachindra Nath** **Sanyal, Ram Prasad Bismil, Ashfaqulla Khan, Jogesh Chandra Chatterjee, and others**. At the time of the Kakori Train Robbery (1925), the organisation did not include Bhagat Singh, who joined later and was part of the reorganisation of the group into the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA) in 1928. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The Yugantar Group was an early 20th-century Bengal revolutionary organisation associated with figures like Barindra Kumar Ghosh and Bagha Jatin. **It was not led by Sachindra Nath Sanyal,** who was instead associated with the HRA in North India. Furthermore, the Chittagong Armoury Raid network of the 1920s was connected with Surya Sen (Masterda), not Yugantar’s older network. **So, pair II is not** **correctly matched.**
● **The Kirti Kisan Party** was a Punjab-based organisation primarily associated with **Sohan Singh Josh** **and** the Ghadarite communists, not with Surya Sen or any Bengal revolutionary network. Its main activities included **propagating socialist and communist ideology among peasants and workers** through the journal Kirti. While the description of its ideological activities is correct, the leadership attribution to Surya Sen is incorrect—Surya Sen was the leader of the Chittagong revolutionary group in Bengal. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● After 1922, revolutionary groups began adopting socialist ideas, significantly influenced by the Russian Revolution.
● The shift from HRA to HSRA (Hindustan Socialist Republican Association) in 1928 reflected this ideological transformation.
● Bipan Chandra notes that the revolutionaries of the 1920s placed increasing emphasis on organisation-building, political education, and ideological clarity rather than relying solely on individual heroic actions.
● Bengal, Punjab, and United Provinces formed the three main centres of revolutionary resurgence in this period.
**Relevance:** Recently, several state governments and the central government have announced memorial projects, museum upgrades, and public lectures honouring revolutionaries such as Ram Prasad Bismil, Ashfaqulla Khan, and Surya Sen.

questions.subject: History
questions.questionText:
